summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: What is Environmental Science?
summary_sections.content_html:
What is Environmental Science?

Environmental science is the interdisciplinary study of how humans interact with the environment. It integrates biology, chemistry, physics, geology, economics, and social sciences to understand and solve environmental problems.

Key Themes

  • Science and the environment: Using the scientific method to study natural systems.
  • Human impact: Agriculture, industry, urbanization, and resource consumption alter ecosystems.
  • Sustainability: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.

flashcards.definition: Interdisciplinary study of how humans interact with the natural environment.
Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs (Brundtland definition).
Caused or influenced by human activity (e.g., anthropogenic climate change).
A community of organisms interacting with their physical environment as a functional unit.
Systematic process: observation → hypothesis → experiment → analysis → conclusion.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Brundtland definition of sustainability states:

quiz_questions.question_text: Rachel Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) focused on:

options.text: The harmful effects of pesticides (DDT) on ecosystems and human health

quiz_questions.question_text: A hypothesis must be:
Controlled experiments in environmental science are often difficult because:

options.text: Environmental systems are complex with many interacting variables difficult to isolate

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental indicators include:

quiz_questions.question_text: The precautionary principle states that:

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental science addresses problems at _____ scale(s).

options.text: Local, regional, national, and global scales

quiz_questions.question_text: An environmental worldview is:

options.text: A person's set of assumptions about the world that shapes their relationship with the environment

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding environmental science is important because:

options.text: Human survival depends on maintaining functional ecosystems, clean air/water, and sustainable resource use

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Interdisciplinary Nature of the Field
summary_sections.content_html:
Interdisciplinary Nature of the Field

Environmental science draws on multiple disciplines:

  • Biology/Ecology: Species interactions, ecosystems, biodiversity.
  • Chemistry: Pollutant behavior, biogeochemical cycles, water chemistry.
  • Physics: Energy flow, thermodynamics, climate dynamics.
  • Geology: Soil science, mineral resources, natural hazards.
  • Social Sciences: Economics (cost-benefit), policy, ethics, demographics.
  • Engineering: Pollution control, renewable energy technology, sustainable design.

flashcards.definition: Study of interactions between organisms and their environment; a foundational discipline of environmental science.
Study of harmful effects of chemicals on organisms; key for pollution assessment.
Applies economic principles to environmental issues (cost-benefit analysis, externalities, valuation).
Philosophical examination of moral relationships between humans and the natural world.
Geographic Information Systems; spatial analysis tools for mapping and analyzing environmental data.

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental science integrates at least _____ major disciplines.

options.text: Multiple (biology, chemistry, physics, geology, social sciences, engineering)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecology contributes to environmental science by:

options.text: Understanding species interactions, energy flow, and ecosystem dynamics

quiz_questions.question_text: Chemistry is essential for understanding:

options.text: Pollutant behavior, biogeochemical cycles, water quality, and atmospheric chemistry

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental economics addresses:

options.text: Externalities (hidden costs of pollution), cost-benefit analysis, and ecosystem services valuation

quiz_questions.question_text: An externality is:

options.text: A cost or benefit that affects parties not directly involved in a transaction (e.g., factory pollution affecting neighbors)

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental ethics asks questions like:

options.text: Do other species have intrinsic value? What responsibilities do we have to future generations?

quiz_questions.question_text: GIS is used in environmental science to:

options.text: Map habitats, track pollution, model land use, analyze spatial patterns, and plan conservation

quiz_questions.question_text: Toxicology helps environmental scientists:

options.text: Assess the harmful effects of chemicals on organisms and determine safe exposure levels

quiz_questions.question_text: LD50 is a toxicology measure representing:

options.text: The dose at which 50% of test organisms die — a standard measure of acute toxicity

quiz_questions.question_text: Bioaccumulation occurs when:

options.text: An organism absorbs a substance faster than it can eliminate it, building up concentration over time

quiz_questions.question_text: Biomagnification refers to:

options.text: Increasing concentration of toxins at higher trophic levels in a food chain

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental policy draws on:

options.text: Science, economics, law, ethics, and public opinion to create regulations and incentives

quiz_questions.question_text: Remote sensing helps environmental scientists by:

options.text: Providing satellite and aerial data on land use, deforestation, ocean health, and atmospheric conditions

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental engineering designs:

options.text: Solutions to environmental problems: water treatment, pollution control, waste management, renewable energy systems

quiz_questions.question_text: Demographic data is important because:

options.text: Population size, growth, and distribution directly affect resource use, pollution, and habitat destruction

quiz_questions.question_text: Soil science (pedology) is critical for:

options.text: Understanding agriculture, erosion, nutrient cycling, water filtration, and carbon storage

quiz_questions.question_text: The interdisciplinary approach is necessary because:

options.text: Simple problems only need one field
Environmental problems are complex, with interconnected ecological, economic, and social dimensions

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental justice combines:

options.text: Social science, ethics, and environmental science to address disproportionate environmental burdens on marginalized communities

quiz_questions.question_text: Systems thinking in environmental science means:

options.text: Understanding interconnections, feedback loops, and emergent properties of complex environmental systems

quiz_questions.question_text: The value of interdisciplinary study is demonstrated by:

options.text: Complex problems like climate change requiring expertise from ALL disciplines to understand and solve

quiz_questions.explanation: Climate change is the ultimate interdisciplinary challenge.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ecosystem Basics (Producers, Consumers, Decomposers)
summary_sections.content_html:
Ecosystem Basics

Trophic Levels

  • Producers (autotrophs): Photosynthesizers (plants, algae, cyanobacteria) and chemosynthesizers.
  • Primary consumers (herbivores): Eat producers (deer, grasshoppers, zooplankton).
  • Secondary consumers: Eat primary consumers (frogs, small fish).
  • Tertiary consumers: Top predators (hawks, sharks, lions).
  • Decomposers: Bacteria and fungi break down dead matter, recycling nutrients.

10% Rule: Approximately 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels; ~90% lost as heat.

flashcards.definition: Organisms that make their own food from inorganic matter (photosynthesis or chemosynthesis).
Organisms that obtain energy by eating other organisms.
Organisms (bacteria, fungi) that break down dead organic matter, recycling nutrients back to the ecosystem.
A feeding position in a food chain (producer = level 1, primary consumer = level 2, etc.).
Approximately 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next; ~90% lost as metabolic heat.

quiz_questions.question_text: Producers are also called:
Photosynthesis converts:
Primary consumers are:
A food chain typically has _____ trophic levels because of energy loss.
If a producer has 10,000 kcal of energy, how much is available to tertiary consumers?
Decomposers are essential because they:

options.text: Recycle nutrients from dead matter back into the soil and atmosphere for reuse

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of a decomposer is:
Detritivores differ from decomposers in that they:

options.text: Physically break down dead material by consuming it (e.g., earthworms, millipedes) while decomposers chemically break it down

quiz_questions.question_text: In a food web, organisms can occupy:

options.text: Multiple trophic levels (omnivores eat at different levels)

quiz_questions.question_text: Net primary productivity (NPP) is:

options.text: Gross primary productivity minus plant respiration — the energy available to consumers

quiz_questions.question_text: Which biome has the highest NPP?

options.text: Tropical rainforest (and estuaries/coral reefs in aquatic systems)

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy enters most ecosystems through:
Chemosynthesis occurs in:

options.text: Deep-sea hydrothermal vents and other light-deprived environments

quiz_questions.question_text: An apex predator is:

options.text: A top predator with no natural predators (e.g., wolves, great white sharks, eagles)

quiz_questions.question_text: Keystone species have disproportionately large effects because:

options.text: Removing them causes significant ecosystem changes despite their relatively small population

quiz_questions.question_text: Trophic cascades occur when:

options.text: Changes at one trophic level ripple through the food web (e.g., removing wolves affects elk affects vegetation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Biomass pyramids are usually widest at the _____ level.

options.text: Bottom (producers have the most biomass in most ecosystems)

quiz_questions.question_text: In aquatic ecosystems, phytoplankton serve as:

options.text: The primary producers (photosynthesizers forming the base of oceanic food webs)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 10% rule means food chains are energy-_____ systems.

options.text: Inefficient (90% of energy is lost as heat at each transfer)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding trophic structure is essential for:

options.text: Conservation planning, fisheries management, pollution assessment, and ecosystem health evaluation

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Biogeochemical Cycles (Carbon, Nitrogen, Water)
summary_sections.content_html:
Biogeochemical Cycles

Carbon Cycle

Photosynthesis (removes CO₂), respiration/decomposition (releases CO₂), fossil fuel combustion, ocean absorption. Human burning of fossil fuels accelerates atmospheric CO₂ (pre-industrial ~280 ppm → 2024 ~425 ppm).

Nitrogen Cycle

N₂ (78% of atmosphere) → fixed by bacteria/lightning → NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ (usable by plants) → consumed by animals → decomposition/denitrification returns N₂. Haber-Bosch process produces synthetic fertilizer.

Water Cycle

Evaporation → condensation → precipitation → runoff/infiltration → groundwater → transpiration. Human impacts: damming, irrigation, groundwater depletion, pollution.

flashcards.definition: Movement of carbon through atmosphere, biosphere, oceans, and lithosphere; photosynthesis and respiration are key processes.
Conversion of atmospheric N₂ into usable forms (NH₃) by bacteria or the Haber-Bosch process.
Conversion of nitrates back to N₂ gas by bacteria; returns nitrogen to the atmosphere.
Continuous movement of water: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, transpiration.
Industrial process converting N₂ + H₂ into ammonia (NH₃) for fertilizer; doubled global food production.

quiz_questions.question_text: The carbon cycle includes all EXCEPT:
Photosynthesis removes _____ from the atmosphere.
Burning fossil fuels releases carbon that was stored:

options.text: Millions of years ago (ancient organisms converted to coal, oil, gas)

quiz_questions.question_text: Pre-industrial CO₂ was ~280 ppm. Current levels are approximately:
The largest carbon reservoir on Earth is:

options.text: The ocean (dissolved CO₂ and carbonate) and sedimentary rocks

quiz_questions.question_text: In the nitrogen cycle, nitrogen fixation converts:

options.text: N₂ (atmospheric gas) to NH₃ (ammonia, usable by organisms)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Haber-Bosch process is significant because:

options.text: It produces synthetic ammonia fertilizer, enabling modern agriculture to feed billions

quiz_questions.question_text: Excess nitrogen fertilizer in waterways causes:

options.text: Eutrophication (algal blooms → oxygen depletion → dead zones)

quiz_questions.question_text: Denitrification returns nitrogen to the atmosphere as:

options.text: N₂ gas (and some N₂O, a greenhouse gas)

quiz_questions.question_text: In the water cycle, transpiration is:

options.text: Water vapor released by plants through their leaves (stomata)

quiz_questions.question_text: Groundwater is accessed by:

options.text: Wells that tap aquifers (underground water-bearing layers)

quiz_questions.question_text: Aquifer depletion occurs when:

options.text: Groundwater is pumped out faster than it's recharged

quiz_questions.question_text: The phosphorus cycle differs from carbon and nitrogen because:

options.text: Phosphorus has no significant atmospheric phase — it cycles through rocks, soil, water, and organisms

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean acidification is caused by:

options.text: CO₂ dissolving in seawater forming carbonic acid (lowering pH)

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon sinks include:

options.text: Forests, oceans, and soil (they absorb more carbon than they release)

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon sources include:

options.text: Fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, and volcanic eruptions (net CO₂ release)

quiz_questions.question_text: Nitrification is the conversion of:

options.text: NH₃ (ammonia) to NO₂⁻ then NO₃⁻ (nitrate) by bacteria

quiz_questions.question_text: Human impacts on the water cycle include:

options.text: Damming rivers, irrigation, groundwater depletion, deforestation, and pollution

quiz_questions.question_text: Biogeochemical cycles are called 'cycles' because:

options.text: Matter is continuously recycled through different forms and reservoirs — it's not created or destroyed

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding biogeochemical cycles is critical for AP because:

options.text: They underpin pollution, climate change, agriculture, and ecosystem health — core AP topics

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sustainability Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Sustainability Concepts

Three Pillars

  • Environmental: Maintaining healthy ecosystems, biodiversity, and natural resources.
  • Economic: Long-term economic viability without depleting natural capital.
  • Social: Equity, justice, health, education, and community well-being.

Key Metrics

  • Ecological footprint, carbon footprint, IPAT equation (Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology).
  • Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): 17 UN goals for 2030.

flashcards.definition: Environmental, economic, and social dimensions — all must be balanced for true sustainability.
Measure of how much biologically productive land and water is needed to support a population's consumption.
Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology; framework for understanding human environmental impact.
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals; 17 goals for 2030 addressing poverty, health, environment, and more.
The world's stock of natural resources (air, water, soil, biodiversity) that provide ecosystem services.

quiz_questions.question_text: The three pillars of sustainability are:
The IPAT equation states that environmental impact equals:
Ecological footprint measures:

options.text: The biologically productive area needed to support a given population's resource consumption and waste absorption

quiz_questions.question_text: If humanity's ecological footprint exceeds Earth's biocapacity, we are in:

options.text: Ecological overshoot (using resources faster than they regenerate)

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth Overshoot Day marks when:

options.text: Humanity has used all the biological resources Earth regenerates in that year

quiz_questions.question_text: The UN's Sustainable Development Goals include _____ goals.
Natural capital refers to:

options.text: Earth's stock of natural resources that provide valuable ecosystem services

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecosystem services include:

options.text: Pollination, water purification, carbon sequestration, flood control, and recreation

quiz_questions.question_text: A carbon footprint measures:

options.text: Total greenhouse gas emissions caused by an individual, organization, or product (in CO₂ equivalents)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'T' in IPAT can reduce impact when:

options.text: Technology improves efficiency (e.g., more food per acre, cleaner energy per unit)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cradle-to-cradle design means:

options.text: Products are designed so all materials can be recycled or composted — no waste

quiz_questions.question_text: The circular economy contrasts with the linear economy by:

options.text: Reusing, repairing, and recycling materials instead of the 'take-make-dispose' model

quiz_questions.question_text: Life cycle assessment (LCA) evaluates:

options.text: Environmental impact of a product from raw material extraction through disposal (cradle to grave)

quiz_questions.question_text: Greenwashing is:

options.text: Misleading marketing that makes products appear more environmentally friendly than they are

quiz_questions.question_text: Per capita ecological footprint is highest in:

options.text: High-income countries (US, Australia, Gulf states) due to high consumption

quiz_questions.question_text: Carrying capacity relates to sustainability because:

options.text: Exceeding carrying capacity leads to resource depletion, ecosystem degradation, and population decline

quiz_questions.question_text: Triple bottom line accounting considers:

options.text: Profit, people, and planet (economic, social, environmental performance)

quiz_questions.question_text: Renewable resources are sustainable only if:

options.text: They are used at or below their rate of regeneration

quiz_questions.question_text: Intergenerational equity means:

options.text: Ensuring future generations have access to the same or better resources and environmental quality

quiz_questions.question_text: Sustainability concepts are foundational to AP Environmental Science because:

options.text: Nearly every AP topic connects back to sustainable resource use and human-environment interactions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Environmental Issues
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Environmental Issues

Dust Bowl (1930s)

Overplowing Great Plains → topsoil loss → massive dust storms. Led to soil conservation practices and the Soil Conservation Service.

Aral Sea Shrinkage

Soviet-era irrigation diverted rivers → Aral Sea shrank ~90%. Fisheries collapsed, salt/dust storms, health crises.

Ozone Hole

CFCs destroyed stratospheric ozone → UV radiation risk. Montreal Protocol (1987) phased out CFCs; ozone recovering.

flashcards.definition: 1930s ecological disaster caused by drought and overplowing; massive topsoil erosion on the Great Plains.
Once the 4th-largest lake; shrank ~90% due to Soviet irrigation diversions; ecological and health disaster.
Seasonal thinning of stratospheric ozone over Antarctica caused by CFCs; allows harmful UV radiation.
1987 international agreement phasing out ozone-depleting substances (CFCs); widely considered a success.
Chlorofluorocarbons; industrial chemicals that destroy stratospheric ozone; banned under the Montreal Protocol.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Dust Bowl was primarily caused by:

options.text: A combination of severe drought and human overplowing that destroyed native grassland

quiz_questions.question_text: The Dust Bowl led to creation of the:

options.text: Soil Conservation Service (now NRCS) and conservation farming practices

quiz_questions.question_text: The Aral Sea shrank because:

options.text: Soviet irrigation projects diverted the rivers feeding it

quiz_questions.question_text: Consequences of the Aral Sea's shrinkage include:

options.text: Fishery collapse, toxic dust storms (pesticides in exposed seabed), and severe health problems

quiz_questions.question_text: Stratospheric ozone depletion is caused primarily by:
The Montreal Protocol (1987) is considered successful because:

options.text: CFC production dropped dramatically and the ozone layer is showing signs of recovery

quiz_questions.question_text: The ozone hole is most severe over:

options.text: Antarctica (due to polar vortex conditions that concentrate ozone-depleting reactions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Love Canal (1970s) was a case of:

options.text: Hazardous chemical waste buried underground contaminating a residential neighborhood in New York

quiz_questions.question_text: The Cuyahoga River (Cleveland) caught fire in 1969 due to:

options.text: Severe industrial pollution (oil and chemical contamination)

quiz_questions.question_text: Bhopal disaster (1984, India) involved:

options.text: A toxic gas leak (methyl isocyanate) from a Union Carbide pesticide plant — thousands died

quiz_questions.question_text: Chernobyl (1986) demonstrated:

options.text: The catastrophic potential of nuclear accidents — radiation contaminated large areas of Europe

quiz_questions.question_text: The Deepwater Horizon spill (2010) released ~4.9 million barrels of oil into:
These case studies share the common lesson that:

options.text: Prevention is far more effective and cheaper than cleanup after environmental damage occurs

quiz_questions.question_text: The success of the Montreal Protocol suggests that:

options.text: When there is clear scientific evidence and political will, international environmental agreements can succeed

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental impact assessments (EIAs) were developed to:

options.text: Evaluate potential environmental effects BEFORE major projects proceed

quiz_questions.question_text: The Flint water crisis (2014–2019) involved:

options.text: Lead contamination of drinking water due to cost-cutting measures that corroded old pipes

quiz_questions.question_text: The Amazon deforestation case shows:

options.text: Clearing for agriculture and cattle ranching destroys biodiversity, carbon storage, and indigenous communities

quiz_questions.question_text: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch illustrates:

options.text: The accumulation of plastic waste in ocean gyres — a growing pollution crisis

quiz_questions.question_text: Learning from case studies helps because:

options.text: Understanding past successes and failures guides better environmental policy and decision-making

quiz_questions.question_text: These case studies demonstrate the importance of:

options.text: Early action, scientific evidence, international cooperation, and holding polluters accountable

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Systems Thinking
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Systems Thinking

Key Concepts

  • Open vs. closed systems: Open exchange matter and energy (most ecosystems); closed exchange only energy (Earth for matter, approximately).
  • Feedback loops: Positive (amplifying: ice melt → less reflection → more warming) and negative (stabilizing: predator-prey cycles).
  • Inputs, outputs, throughputs: Energy and matter flow through systems.
  • Emergent properties: Properties arising from system interactions that individual components don't possess.

flashcards.definition: System that exchanges both matter and energy with its surroundings (e.g., an ecosystem).
System that exchanges energy but not matter with its surroundings (Earth, approximately).
Amplifying loop: output reinforces the change (e.g., ice-albedo feedback accelerating warming).
Stabilizing loop: output counteracts the change (e.g., predator-prey regulation).
Properties of a system that arise from interactions of its parts and don't exist in individual components.

quiz_questions.question_text: An open system exchanges:

options.text: Both matter and energy with its surroundings

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth is approximately a closed system for:

options.text: Matter (very little matter enters or leaves; energy from Sun enters, heat radiates out)

quiz_questions.explanation: Sunlight in, heat out; matter stays.

quiz_questions.question_text: A positive feedback loop:

options.text: Amplifies a change — the output reinforces the initial change (e.g., more warming → more ice melt → more warming)

quiz_questions.question_text: A negative feedback loop:

options.text: Counteracts a change — the output opposes the initial change, stabilizing the system

quiz_questions.question_text: The ice-albedo feedback is a _____ feedback loop.

options.text: Positive (ice melts → darker surface → absorbs more heat → more ice melts)

quiz_questions.question_text: Predator-prey dynamics are an example of _____ feedback.

options.text: Negative (more prey → more predators → fewer prey → fewer predators → more prey)

quiz_questions.question_text: If CO₂ increases temperature, which causes more CO₂ release from oceans, this is:

options.text: Positive feedback (warming → more CO₂ → more warming)

quiz_questions.question_text: Thermoregulation in the human body (sweating when hot) is:

options.text: Negative feedback (body temperature rises → sweating cools body → temperature decreases)

quiz_questions.question_text: Emergent properties means:

options.text: The whole system has properties that individual components lack (e.g., a forest is more than individual trees)

quiz_questions.question_text: In the AP exam, systems thinking questions may involve:

options.text: Identifying feedback loops, predicting system behavior, and analyzing inputs/outputs

quiz_questions.question_text: A tipping point in an environmental system is:

options.text: A threshold beyond which a system shifts to a new state and may not easily return

quiz_questions.question_text: The Earth system includes interconnected _____ (spheres).

options.text: 4 main spheres: atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, and biosphere

quiz_questions.question_text: Steady state in a system means:

options.text: Inputs balance outputs — the system appears stable even though matter/energy flow continuously

quiz_questions.question_text: Lag time in environmental systems means:

options.text: There is a delay between a cause and its full effect (e.g., CO₂ emissions today → warming for decades)

quiz_questions.question_text: Resilience is a system's ability to:

options.text: Absorb disturbance and return to its original state

quiz_questions.question_text: Resistance is a system's ability to:
A systems diagram typically shows:

options.text: Boxes (components), arrows (flows), and feedback loops connecting parts of the system

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP quantitative problems, students may need to:

options.text: Calculate changes in inputs/outputs, trace matter/energy through systems, and predict feedback effects

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'thresholds' is important because:

options.text: Small changes can trigger abrupt, often irreversible shifts when a threshold is crossed

quiz_questions.question_text: Systems thinking is the foundation of APES because:

options.text: Every environmental topic involves interconnected systems, feedback loops, and complex cause-effect relationships

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy Flow in Ecosystems
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy Flow in Ecosystems

Energy flows in one direction through ecosystems: Sun → producers → consumers → heat. It is not recycled.

Key Measures

  • GPP (Gross Primary Productivity): Total energy fixed by photosynthesis.
  • NPP (Net Primary Productivity): GPP minus plant respiration; energy available to consumers.
  • 1st Law of Thermodynamics: Energy is conserved — transferred, not created or destroyed.
  • 2nd Law: Energy conversions increase entropy; energy is lost as heat at each transfer.

flashcards.definition: Gross Primary Productivity; total amount of energy fixed by photosynthesis in an ecosystem.
Net Primary Productivity; GPP minus plant respiration (R); energy available to consumers. NPP = GPP - R.
Energy is conserved; it can be transformed but never created or destroyed.
Every energy conversion increases entropy; energy is lost as heat (explains the 10% rule).
One-way movement of energy through trophic levels; unlike matter, energy is not recycled in ecosystems.

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy flows through ecosystems in:

options.text: One direction (Sun → producers → consumers → heat; it is not recycled)

quiz_questions.question_text: GPP equals:

options.text: Total energy fixed by all photosynthesis in an ecosystem

quiz_questions.question_text: NPP is calculated as:

options.text: GPP − R (gross primary productivity minus plant respiration)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2nd law of thermodynamics explains why:

options.text: Energy is lost as heat at each trophic transfer (the 10% rule)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 10% rule states that approximately _____ of energy transfers to the next trophic level.
If producers fix 20,000 kcal/m²/yr and respire 12,000, NPP is:
Which biome has the highest NPP?
Which aquatic ecosystem has very high productivity?
Open ocean has low NPP per unit area but high total NPP because:

options.text: It covers the largest area on Earth

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy available at the tertiary consumer level from 10,000 kcal at the producer level is approximately:
Assimilation efficiency is the fraction of ingested energy that:

options.text: Is absorbed into the body (not lost as feces)

quiz_questions.question_text: Production efficiency is the fraction of assimilated energy that:

options.text: Is converted to new biomass (growth + reproduction)

quiz_questions.question_text: Why are there rarely more than 5 trophic levels?

options.text: Insufficient energy remains to support higher levels after successive 90% losses
No species exist to fill them

quiz_questions.question_text: An energy pyramid is always upright because:

options.text: Energy decreases at each higher trophic level (never increases due to thermodynamics)

quiz_questions.question_text: A biomass pyramid can sometimes be inverted in:

options.text: Aquatic systems where fast-reproducing phytoplankton are consumed quickly but have low standing biomass

quiz_questions.question_text: Chemosynthetic bacteria contribute to energy flow in:

options.text: Deep-sea hydrothermal vents and other dark environments

quiz_questions.question_text: Global NPP is roughly evenly split between:
Climate change affects NPP by:

options.text: Changing temperature, precipitation, CO₂ levels, and growing seasons — effects vary by region

quiz_questions.question_text: Measuring NPP helps scientists:

options.text: Assess ecosystem health, estimate food production capacity, and track global carbon cycling

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding energy flow is essential for AP because it connects:

options.text: Trophic levels, nutrient cycling, biodiversity, and human impacts on ecosystems

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Food Chains & Food Webs
summary_sections.content_html:
Food Chains & Food Webs

Food chain: Linear sequence of who eats whom (grass → rabbit → fox → eagle). Food web: Complex, interconnected food chains showing multiple feeding relationships in an ecosystem.

Key Concepts

  • Grazing food chain: Starts with producers (most terrestrial/aquatic systems).
  • Detrital food chain: Starts with dead organic matter (decomposition-based).
  • Keystone species: Removal causes disproportionate ecosystem change.
  • Trophic cascades: Changes at one level ripple through the web.

flashcards.definition: Linear pathway showing energy/nutrient transfer from one organism to the next.
Complex network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem.
Species whose removal causes disproportionately large changes in its ecosystem (e.g., sea otters, wolves).
Ecological process where changes at one trophic level cause reciprocal changes through the food web.
Energy pathway starting with dead organic matter processed by decomposers and detritivores.

quiz_questions.question_text: A food web differs from a food chain because it:

options.text: Shows the complex, interconnected feeding relationships (multiple chains linked together)

quiz_questions.question_text: A grazing food chain starts with:
A detrital food chain starts with:
A keystone species is one whose:

options.text: Removal causes disproportionate ecosystem change relative to its biomass or abundance

quiz_questions.question_text: Sea otters are a keystone species because:

options.text: They control sea urchin populations; without otters, urchins overgraze kelp forests, destroying the habitat

quiz_questions.question_text: The reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone caused:

options.text: A trophic cascade: wolves reduced elk overgrazing → vegetation recovered → stream banks stabilized → biodiversity increased

quiz_questions.question_text: In a food web, removing a species can cause:

options.text: Ripple effects through multiple trophic levels (trophic cascade)

quiz_questions.question_text: Omnivores complicate food webs because they:

options.text: Feed at multiple trophic levels, connecting different food chains

quiz_questions.question_text: The detrital food chain processes approximately _____ of energy in most terrestrial ecosystems.

options.text: Much more than the grazing chain (~80-90% of plant matter enters detrital pathways)

quiz_questions.question_text: Indicator species are organisms that:

options.text: Reflect the health of an ecosystem (e.g., amphibians indicate water quality)

quiz_questions.question_text: Foundation species create habitat that:

options.text: Supports many other species (e.g., coral, kelp forests, mangroves)

quiz_questions.question_text: Invasive species often disrupt food webs by:

options.text: Outcompeting native species, having no natural predators, or altering habitat

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of a invasive species disrupting a food web is:

options.text: Zebra mussels in the Great Lakes (filter water, outcompete natives, alter food web base)

quiz_questions.question_text: Bottom-up control of food webs means:

options.text: Nutrient/producer availability controls populations up the food chain

quiz_questions.question_text: Top-down control means:

options.text: Predators control populations of lower trophic levels (predator-prey regulation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Bioaccumulation refers to:

options.text: Buildup of a persistent substance (e.g., mercury) within an organism over time

quiz_questions.question_text: Biomagnification causes toxin concentrations to _____ at higher trophic levels.

options.text: Increase (each predator accumulates toxins from many prey)

quiz_questions.question_text: DDT biomagnification caused _____ in birds of prey.

options.text: Eggshell thinning (leading to reproductive failure in eagles, pelicans, etc.)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding food webs helps manage:

options.text: Fisheries, conservation, pest control, and pollution (tracing toxins through trophic levels)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should be able to:

options.text: Trace energy flow, predict trophic cascade effects, and analyze food web disruptions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Population Dynamics
summary_sections.content_html:
Population Dynamics

Growth Models

  • Exponential growth: J-curve; unlimited resources; dN/dt = rN (r = birth rate − death rate).
  • Logistic growth: S-curve; limited resources; dN/dt = rN(1 − N/K) where K = carrying capacity.

Strategies

  • r-selected: Many offspring, little parental care, rapid growth (bacteria, insects).
  • K-selected: Few offspring, extensive parental care, slow growth (elephants, whales).

Regulation

Density-dependent (competition, predation, disease) and density-independent (weather, fire, flood) factors.

flashcards.definition: J-curve; population grows without limit: dN/dt = rN; occurs with unlimited resources.
S-curve; growth slows as population approaches carrying capacity: dN/dt = rN(1 − N/K).
Maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources.
Species producing many offspring with little parental care; rapid growth; short lifespan (insects, bacteria).
Species producing few offspring with extensive parental care; slow growth; long lifespan (elephants, whales).

quiz_questions.question_text: Exponential growth produces a:

options.text: J-curve (population increases without limit when resources are abundant)

quiz_questions.question_text: The logistic growth equation is:
Carrying capacity (K) is defined as:

options.text: The maximum population size an environment can sustain given available resources

quiz_questions.question_text: As a population approaches K, growth rate:
r-selected species are characterized by:

options.text: Many offspring, little parental care, rapid maturation

quiz_questions.question_text: K-selected species are characterized by:

options.text: Few offspring, parental care, slow maturation, long lifespan

quiz_questions.question_text: Density-dependent limiting factors include:

options.text: Competition, predation, disease, and parasitism (intensify as population grows)

quiz_questions.question_text: Density-independent limiting factors include:

options.text: Natural disasters (fire, flood, drought, extreme weather)

quiz_questions.question_text: If r = 0.05 and N = 200, exponential growth rate (dN/dt) is:
If r = 0.1, N = 500, and K = 1000, logistic growth rate is:
Maximum logistic growth rate occurs at:
Overshoot occurs when a population:

options.text: Temporarily exceeds K, often followed by a die-off

quiz_questions.question_text: A population crash after overshoot can reduce the population:

options.text: Well below K (and sometimes below a sustainable level)

quiz_questions.question_text: Survivorship curves come in three types. Type I shows:

options.text: Low mortality until old age (humans, elephants — K-selected)

quiz_questions.question_text: Type III survivorship shows:

options.text: Very high early mortality with few survivors living long (fish, many invertebrates — r-selected)

quiz_questions.question_text: Lag phase in population growth occurs because:

options.text: Small initial populations take time to reproduce and accumulate numbers

quiz_questions.question_text: Biotic potential is:

options.text: The maximum rate of increase (rmax) under ideal conditions — rarely achieved in nature

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental resistance includes all factors that:

options.text: Limit population growth and keep it below biotic potential (predation, disease, competition, limited resources)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding population dynamics is essential for:

options.text: Wildlife management, pest control, conservation biology, fisheries, and human population planning

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, students should calculate growth rates using:

options.text: Both exponential (dN/dt = rN) and logistic (dN/dt = rN(1-N/K)) equations

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Community Interactions (Competition, Symbiosis)
summary_sections.content_html:
Community Interactions

Types

  • Competition: Interspecific (between species) and intraspecific (within species). Competitive exclusion principle — two species can't occupy the same niche indefinitely.
  • Predation: One organism kills and eats another; drives coevolution (camouflage, warning coloration).
  • Symbiosis: Mutualism (+/+), commensalism (+/0), parasitism (+/−).
  • Niche partitioning: Species reduce competition by using different parts of the same resource.

flashcards.definition: Two species competing for the same limited resource cannot coexist indefinitely; one will be eliminated.
Species reduce competition by using different parts of a resource (e.g., different tree heights for feeding).
Symbiotic relationship benefiting both species (+/+); e.g., bees and flowers.
Symbiotic relationship where one benefits at the other's expense (+/−); e.g., ticks on deer.
Reciprocal evolutionary change between interacting species; e.g., predator-prey arms race.

quiz_questions.question_text: Interspecific competition occurs between:

options.text: Individuals of different species competing for the same resources

quiz_questions.question_text: The competitive exclusion principle states that:

options.text: Two species cannot occupy the same niche indefinitely — one will outcompete the other

quiz_questions.question_text: Niche partitioning allows species to coexist by:

options.text: Using different parts of a resource (e.g., feeding at different heights, times, or locations)

quiz_questions.question_text: An ecological niche includes:

options.text: An organism's role, habitat, feeding behavior, interactions, and all conditions for survival

quiz_questions.question_text: A fundamental niche is _____ than a realized niche.

options.text: Larger (the full range of conditions a species could occupy vs. what it actually occupies due to competition)

quiz_questions.question_text: Mutualism means:

options.text: Both species benefit from the interaction (+/+)

quiz_questions.question_text: Example of mutualism:

options.text: Mycorrhizal fungi and plant roots (fungi provide nutrients, plant provides sugars)

quiz_questions.question_text: Commensalism means:

options.text: One species benefits while the other is unaffected (+/0)

quiz_questions.question_text: Example of commensalism:

options.text: Barnacles on a whale (barnacles benefit from transport/feeding; whale unaffected)

quiz_questions.question_text: Parasitism means:

options.text: One species benefits at the expense of the other (+/−)

quiz_questions.question_text: Coevolution between predators and prey results in:

options.text: An evolutionary arms race (faster prey → faster predators → faster prey)

quiz_questions.question_text: Mimicry is an example of:

options.text: Predator-prey coevolution (Batesian: harmless mimics harmful; Müllerian: harmful species resemble each other)

quiz_questions.question_text: Batesian mimicry is when a _____ species mimics a harmful one.

options.text: Harmless (it gains protection by looking like something predators avoid)

quiz_questions.question_text: Müllerian mimicry is when two _____ species resemble each other.

options.text: Harmful (predators learn to avoid the shared warning pattern more quickly)

quiz_questions.question_text: Amensalism (0/−) occurs when:

options.text: One species is harmed while the other is unaffected (e.g., large tree shading out small plants)

quiz_questions.question_text: Herbivory is a form of:

options.text: Predation (plant is harmed when consumed by an herbivore, though usually not killed)

quiz_questions.question_text: r-selected species typically experience _____ density-dependent regulation.

options.text: Weak (their populations often crash from density-independent factors before reaching K)

quiz_questions.question_text: K-selected species are more affected by _____ factors.

options.text: Density-dependent (competition, disease become critical near carrying capacity)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding community interactions helps manage:

options.text: Invasive species, conservation of endangered species, biological pest control, and ecosystem restoration

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should identify interaction types and predict outcomes of:

options.text: Species additions, removals, and environmental changes on community structure

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Succession (Primary & Secondary)
summary_sections.content_html:
Ecological Succession

Primary Succession

Occurs on bare substrate with no soil (lava flows, retreating glaciers). Pioneer species (lichens, mosses) colonize first → soil develops → grasses → shrubs → trees → climax community. Takes centuries to millennia.

Secondary Succession

Occurs after disturbance that leaves soil intact (fire, logging, abandonment). Faster than primary — soil, seeds, and organisms remain. Grasses/weeds → shrubs → trees → climax community.

flashcards.definition: Ecological succession starting on bare substrate with no soil; very slow (centuries).
Succession after disturbance that leaves soil intact; faster than primary (decades).
First organisms to colonize bare substrate (lichens, mosses in primary; weeds/grasses in secondary).
The relatively stable ecosystem that develops at the end of ecological succession.
An event that disrupts an ecosystem (fire, flood, storm, volcanic eruption, human activity).

quiz_questions.question_text: Primary succession begins on:

options.text: Bare rock or substrate with no soil (e.g., after volcanic eruption or glacier retreat)

quiz_questions.question_text: Pioneer species in primary succession are typically:

options.text: Lichens and mosses that can colonize bare rock and begin soil formation

quiz_questions.question_text: Secondary succession occurs after:

options.text: A disturbance that leaves soil intact (fire, farming abandonment, logging)

quiz_questions.question_text: Secondary succession is _____ than primary succession.

options.text: Faster (soil, seeds, and root systems already present)

quiz_questions.question_text: A climax community is:

options.text: A relatively stable, mature community that persists until the next major disturbance

quiz_questions.question_text: Intermediate disturbance hypothesis states that:

options.text: Moderate disturbance levels maintain highest biodiversity

quiz_questions.question_text: Facilitation in succession means:

options.text: Early species modify the environment to make it more suitable for later species

quiz_questions.question_text: Inhibition in succession means:

options.text: Early species resist replacement by making conditions less favorable for newcomers

quiz_questions.question_text: Tolerance in succession means:

options.text: Later species can grow regardless of early species' presence (they tolerate existing conditions)

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of primary succession is:

options.text: Colonization of a newly formed volcanic island (e.g., Surtsey, Iceland)

quiz_questions.question_text: An example of secondary succession is:

options.text: Regrowth of a forest after a wildfire

quiz_questions.question_text: Old-field succession (abandoned farmland) is a type of:

options.text: Secondary succession (soil exists; weeds → grasses → shrubs → trees over decades)

quiz_questions.question_text: Biodiversity typically _____ during succession until the climax stage.

options.text: Increases (more species diversity as the community develops)

quiz_questions.question_text: In modern ecology, the concept of 'climax community' is considered:

options.text: More of a general trend than a fixed endpoint — communities are dynamic and change continuously

quiz_questions.question_text: Fire-adapted ecosystems (chaparral, prairies) may depend on periodic fires because:

options.text: Fire prevents woody plant encroachment, recycles nutrients, and stimulates seed germination

quiz_questions.question_text: Invasive species can disrupt succession by:

options.text: Outcompeting native colonizers and preventing normal community development

quiz_questions.question_text: Soil formation during primary succession involves:

options.text: Physical weathering of rock, decomposition of pioneer organisms, and accumulation of organic matter

quiz_questions.question_text: Nurse logs in forest succession help because:

options.text: Decomposing logs provide nutrients, moisture, and elevated growing surfaces for seedlings

quiz_questions.question_text: Aquatic succession (e.g., lake filling in) can turn a lake into:

options.text: A meadow or forest over long periods as sediment accumulates

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding succession is important for AP because it connects to:

options.text: Biodiversity, disturbance ecology, conservation, restoration ecology, and ecosystem management

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Biodiversity & Conservation
summary_sections.content_html:
Biodiversity & Conservation

Levels of Biodiversity

  • Genetic: Variation within a species (allele diversity).
  • Species: Number of different species in an area.
  • Ecosystem: Variety of habitats, communities, and ecological processes.

Threats

HIPPCO: Habitat destruction, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation. Currently in the 6th mass extinction (species loss rate 100–1,000× background).

flashcards.definition: Variety of life at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels.
Threats to biodiversity: Habitat destruction, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation.
Species in danger of extinction throughout all or a significant portion of its range.
Breaking continuous habitat into smaller, isolated patches; reduces biodiversity and gene flow.
Current ongoing extinction event driven by human activities; loss rate 100–1,000× the natural background rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: The three levels of biodiversity are:
HIPPCO stands for:

options.text: Habitat destruction, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation

quiz_questions.question_text: The leading cause of biodiversity loss is:

options.text: Habitat destruction (deforestation, urbanization, agriculture converting natural areas)

quiz_questions.question_text: Habitat fragmentation harms species by:

options.text: Isolating populations, reducing genetic diversity, increasing edge effects, and limiting migration

quiz_questions.question_text: Edge effects in fragmented habitats include:

options.text: Increased exposure to wind, temperature changes, predators, and invasive species at habitat boundaries

quiz_questions.question_text: Invasive species threaten biodiversity because:

options.text: They outcompete natives, have no natural predators, and can alter habitat (e.g., kudzu, cane toads)

quiz_questions.question_text: The current extinction rate is estimated at _____ times the natural background rate.
Species richness refers to:

options.text: The total number of different species in an area

quiz_questions.question_text: Species evenness refers to:

options.text: How evenly individuals are distributed among species (high evenness = no single species dominates)

quiz_questions.question_text: An endangered species is one that:

options.text: Is in danger of extinction throughout all or a significant portion of its range

quiz_questions.question_text: The Endangered Species Act (1973, USA) protects:

options.text: Listed plant and animal species and their critical habitats

quiz_questions.question_text: Biodiversity hotspots are regions with:

options.text: High species richness AND high threat of habitat loss (>1,500 endemic plant species; >70% habitat lost)

quiz_questions.question_text: Genetic diversity is important because it:

options.text: Provides variation for adaptation and resilience; low genetic diversity increases extinction risk

quiz_questions.question_text: In-situ conservation means:

options.text: Protecting species in their natural habitat (national parks, reserves, protected areas)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ex-situ conservation means:

options.text: Protecting species outside their natural habitat (zoos, botanical gardens, seed banks, gene banks)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecosystem services lost when biodiversity declines include:

options.text: Pollination, water purification, carbon sequestration, pest control, and nutrient cycling

quiz_questions.question_text: The economic value of ecosystem services is estimated at:

options.text: Trillions of dollars annually ($125+ trillion/year globally by some estimates)

quiz_questions.question_text: Wildlife corridors help biodiversity by:

options.text: Connecting fragmented habitats, allowing gene flow, migration, and access to resources

quiz_questions.question_text: The precautionary principle applied to biodiversity means:

options.text: Take protective action even before full science is complete if biodiversity is at risk

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding biodiversity is essential for AP because:

options.text: It's a core theme connecting ecology, conservation, policy, and human well-being

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Ecology
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Ecology

Yellowstone Wolf Reintroduction (1995)

Wolves reintroduced → elk behavior changed → riparian vegetation recovered → streambank stability → beaver return → trophic cascade demonstrating top-down regulation.

Coral Reef Decline

Warming → coral bleaching (loss of zooxanthellae) → biodiversity collapse. 14% of world's coral lost 2009–2018.

Amazon Deforestation

~17% of Amazon cleared for cattle and soy; approaching tipping point where forest could convert to savanna, releasing massive CO₂.

flashcards.definition: Wolf reintroduction reduced elk overgrazing, allowing vegetation recovery and stream restoration.
Corals expel symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) under heat stress, turning white; can lead to death if prolonged.
Theory that ~20-25% deforestation could trigger irreversible conversion of Amazon forest to savanna.
Symbiotic algae living in coral tissue; provide up to 90% of the coral's energy through photosynthesis.
Region with exceptionally high species richness and endemism that is significantly threatened by human activities.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Yellowstone wolf reintroduction demonstrated:

options.text: A trophic cascade: wolves → changed elk behavior → vegetation recovery → stream restoration

quiz_questions.question_text: Coral bleaching occurs when:

options.text: Elevated water temperatures cause corals to expel their symbiotic zooxanthellae algae

quiz_questions.question_text: Zooxanthellae provide corals with up to _____ of their energy.
If bleaching is prolonged, corals can:
Amazon deforestation is primarily driven by:
The Amazon tipping point concept suggests that at ~20-25% deforestation:

options.text: The forest may irreversibly convert to savanna (reduced rainfall, increased fire susceptibility)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Amazon rainforest produces approximately _____ of its own rainfall through transpiration.
The Great Barrier Reef has experienced mass bleaching events in:

options.text: Multiple times, including severe events in 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024

quiz_questions.question_text: Invasive lionfish in the Atlantic/Caribbean demonstrate:

options.text: How a species with no natural predators can devastate native reef fish populations

quiz_questions.question_text: The Chesapeake Bay has suffered from:

options.text: Eutrophication due to agricultural runoff (nitrogen, phosphorus) causing dead zones

quiz_questions.question_text: Dead zones in the Gulf of Mexico are caused by:

options.text: Agricultural fertilizer runoff from the Mississippi River causing algal blooms → oxygen depletion

quiz_questions.question_text: The passenger pigeon extinction teaches us that:

options.text: Even abundant species can be driven to extinction by overexploitation and habitat loss

quiz_questions.question_text: Northern white rhino conservation demonstrates:

options.text: The difficulty of saving species once populations drop to critically low levels (only 2 females remain as of 2024)

quiz_questions.question_text: The recovery of bald eagles in the US was aided by:

options.text: Banning DDT (1972), Endangered Species Act protection, and habitat conservation

quiz_questions.question_text: These ecology case studies share the lesson that:

options.text: Ecosystems are interconnected — human actions have cascading and often unexpected consequences

quiz_questions.question_text: Conservation success stories show that:

options.text: Species and ecosystems can recover when human threats are reduced and habitats are protected

quiz_questions.question_text: Mangrove destruction increases coastal vulnerability because:

options.text: Mangroves protect coasts from storms, nursery habitat for fish, and sequester carbon

quiz_questions.question_text: Prairie restoration in the US Midwest aims to:

options.text: Restore native grassland ecosystems that support pollinators, store carbon, and prevent erosion

quiz_questions.question_text: These case studies demonstrate the importance of:

options.text: Applying ecological principles to real-world conservation and management challenges

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should be able to analyze case studies by:

options.text: Identifying ecological principles at work, tracing cause-and-effect, and evaluating management approaches

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Population Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Population Models

Calculations

  • Growth rate (r): r = (births − deaths) / N or r = birth rate − death rate.
  • Doubling time: t = 70/r (Rule of 70); r in percent.
  • Exponential: dN/dt = rN.
  • Logistic: dN/dt = rN(1 − N/K). Maximum growth at N = K/2.
  • Population change: ΔN = (births + immigration) − (deaths + emigration).

flashcards.definition: Doubling time = 70 ÷ growth rate (in percent); quick estimation of how fast a population doubles.
Per capita rate of population change; r = birth rate − death rate (for closed populations).
Maximum population growth rate occurs at N = K/2 (inflection point of the S-curve).
ΔN = (births + immigration) − (deaths + emigration); comprehensive population accounting.
Time for a population to double in size; shorter doubling time = faster growth.

options.text: The fraction of carrying capacity still available (environmental resistance)

options.text: r = (births − deaths)/N or birth rate minus death rate

quiz_questions.question_text: A population in decline has r that is:

If crude birth rate = 25/1000 and crude death rate = 10/1000, r =:

options.text: Replacement level fertility (population stabilizes over time)

quiz_questions.question_text: Age structure diagrams with wide bases indicate:

options.text: Rapidly growing populations (many young people who will enter reproductive age)

quiz_questions.question_text: In an AP free-response question about population growth, you should:

options.text: Show calculations, identify the model (exponential vs logistic), state assumptions, and interpret results

quiz_questions.question_text: If a population grows from 500 to 650 in one year with no migration, births were:

options.text: More (competition, disease, and stress intensify at high density)

quiz_questions.question_text: The intrinsic rate of increase (rmax) represents:

options.text: Maximum possible growth rate under ideal conditions (no limiting factors)

quiz_questions.question_text: Mastering population models for AP requires:

options.text: Fluency with equations (exponential, logistic), rule of 70, and interpreting growth curves and age structure diagrams

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Human Population Growth
summary_sections.content_html:
Human Population Growth

World population ~8 billion (2024). Grew slowly for millennia then exponentially after the Industrial Revolution (better agriculture, sanitation, medicine). Current growth rate ~1.0%/yr.

Key Milestones

  • 1 billion: ~1800
  • 2 billion: 1927
  • 4 billion: 1974
  • 8 billion: 2022

Total Fertility Rate (TFR): Average children per woman. Replacement level ≈ 2.1. TFR varies: Sub-Saharan Africa ~4.5; Europe ~1.5.

flashcards.definition: Average number of children born per woman during her lifetime.
TFR of ~2.1 needed to maintain population size without migration.
Human population grew slowly for millennia, then surged exponentially after ~1800 due to the Industrial Revolution.
Tendency for population to continue growing even after fertility drops to replacement, because large young cohorts enter reproductive age.
When birth rate equals death rate and population size stabilizes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The world population reached 8 billion in approximately:
Human population grew exponentially starting around:

options.text: 1800 (Industrial Revolution — improved agriculture, sanitation, and medicine)

quiz_questions.question_text: Replacement level fertility is approximately:
Sub-Saharan Africa's TFR is approximately:
Europe's average TFR is approximately:
The time it took to go from 1 billion to 2 billion was ~_____ years.
Demographic momentum means:

options.text: Population continues growing even after fertility drops because large young cohorts haven't reproduced yet

quiz_questions.question_text: Which factor most contributed to the population explosion?

options.text: Improved public health (sanitation, vaccines, antibiotics) and agricultural advances

quiz_questions.question_text: Current global growth rate is approximately:
The peak global growth rate (~2.2%) occurred in:
Using the Rule of 70, a 1% growth rate means doubling in:
China's one-child policy (1980–2015) aimed to:

options.text: Slow population growth by limiting families to one child

quiz_questions.question_text: India overtook China as the most populous country in:
The demographic transition is driven by:

options.text: Industrialization, urbanization, education (especially women's), and improved healthcare

quiz_questions.question_text: Population projections for 2100 range from about:
Thomas Malthus (1798) warned that:

options.text: Population grows exponentially while food grows arithmetically (leading to inevitable famine)

quiz_questions.question_text: Neo-Malthusians argue that:

options.text: Finite resources limit population growth; without controls, famine and conflict will result

quiz_questions.question_text: Cornucopians argue that:

options.text: Technological innovation and market forces will provide solutions to resource scarcity

quiz_questions.question_text: IPAT equation (I = P × A × T) states that environmental impact depends on:
Understanding human population is critical for AP because it underlies:

options.text: Resource use, pollution, habitat loss, energy demand, and virtually every environmental issue

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Demographic Transition Model
summary_sections.content_html:
Demographic Transition Model (DTM)

Four Stages

  • Stage 1 – Pre-industrial: High birth and death rates → slow/no growth.
  • Stage 2 – Transitional: Death rates drop (better health care); birth rates remain high → rapid growth.
  • Stage 3 – Industrial: Birth rates decline (urbanization, education, family planning) → growth slows.
  • Stage 4 – Post-industrial: Low birth and death rates → slow/zero growth.

Some add Stage 5: Birth rate falls below death rate → population decline (Japan, parts of Europe).

flashcards.definition: Four-stage model showing how populations shift from high birth/death rates to low rates with economic development.
High birth rate + high death rate → little or no population growth.
Death rate drops while birth rate stays high → rapid population growth.
Birth rate declining; death rate low → growth rate slowing.
Low birth + low death rates → slow or zero population growth.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Stage 1 of the DTM, population grows:

options.text: Slowly or not at all (high birth and death rates largely cancel out)

quiz_questions.question_text: Stage 2 sees rapid growth because:

options.text: Death rate drops sharply while birth rate remains high

quiz_questions.question_text: What causes the death rate drop in Stage 2?

options.text: Improved sanitation, medicine, nutrition, and public health

quiz_questions.question_text: In Stage 3, birth rates decline due to:

options.text: Urbanization, education (especially women's), contraception, and economic changes

quiz_questions.question_text: Stage 4 is characterized by:

options.text: Low birth and low death rates → slow or zero population growth

quiz_questions.question_text: A proposed Stage 5 describes:

options.text: Population decline as birth rates fall below death rates (e.g., Japan, Italy, South Korea)

quiz_questions.question_text: Most of Sub-Saharan Africa is currently in:

options.text: Stage 2 or early Stage 3 (high birth rates, declining death rates)

quiz_questions.question_text: Most of Western Europe is in:
The DTM suggests that economic development leads to:

options.text: Lower birth and death rates (population stabilization)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'bulge' in population growth (demographic gap) occurs in:

options.text: Stage 2 (large gap between declining death rate and still-high birth rate)

quiz_questions.question_text: Women's education is strongly correlated with:

options.text: Lower fertility rates (educated women tend to have fewer, healthier children)

quiz_questions.question_text: Family planning programs help by:

options.text: Providing access to contraception and reproductive health education, lowering TFR

quiz_questions.question_text: The DTM has been criticized because:

options.text: It assumes all nations follow the same path; many nations don't fit neatly into the model
All of the above are valid

quiz_questions.question_text: Some countries have 'stalled' in Stage 2/3 due to:

options.text: Poverty, lack of education, political instability, and limited healthcare access

quiz_questions.question_text: Japan's population is declining because:

options.text: TFR (~1.2) is far below replacement (2.1) with aging population

quiz_questions.question_text: An aging population (like Japan) faces challenges including:

options.text: Labor shortages, increased healthcare costs, pension burdens, and reduced economic productivity

quiz_questions.question_text: The dependency ratio compares:

options.text: Non-working-age people (young + old) to working-age population

quiz_questions.question_text: Pronatalist policies (encouraging births) have been adopted by:

options.text: Countries with declining populations like France, Sweden, and South Korea (through incentives, childcare, parental leave)

quiz_questions.question_text: The DTM is useful for AP because it:

options.text: Connects industrialization, public health, urbanization, education, and population dynamics in a testable framework

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding the DTM helps predict:

options.text: Future population trends, resource needs, and environmental impacts for countries at different stages

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Age Structure Diagrams
summary_sections.content_html:
Age Structure Diagrams

Population pyramids show age and sex distribution. Shape predicts future growth:

  • Expansive (wide base): Rapid growth — many young people (Nigeria, Ethiopia).
  • Stationary (column-shaped): Stable — roughly equal numbers at each age (US, Australia).
  • Constrictive (narrow base): Declining — fewer young than middle-aged (Japan, Germany, Italy).

Diagrams reveal dependency ratio and demographic momentum — critical for policy planning.

flashcards.definition: Graph showing population distribution by age and sex; also called population pyramid.
Wide base (many young); indicates rapid population growth.
Column shape (equal ages); indicates stable population.
Narrow base (fewer young); indicates declining population.
Ratio of dependents (age 0–14 and 65+) to working-age population (15–64).

quiz_questions.question_text: An expansive (pyramid-shaped) age structure indicates:

options.text: Rapid population growth (large proportion of young people)

quiz_questions.question_text: A constrictive (urn-shaped) age structure indicates:
A stationary (column-shaped) age structure indicates:

options.text: Relatively stable population (approximately equal numbers at each age)

quiz_questions.question_text: Nigeria's age structure is typically:

options.text: Expansive/pyramid-shaped (very wide base; high proportion of youth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Japan's age structure is typically:

options.text: Constrictive (narrow base; aging population; more elderly than young)

quiz_questions.question_text: Age structure diagrams are split by:

options.text: Sex (males on one side, females on the other, with age groups on the vertical axis)

quiz_questions.question_text: The dependency ratio includes people aged:
A high dependency ratio means:

options.text: Fewer working-age people supporting more dependents → economic strain

quiz_questions.question_text: Demographic momentum is visible in pyramids with:

options.text: Wide bases (large young cohorts will grow into reproductive age even if TFR drops)

quiz_questions.question_text: A country with 40% of its population under age 15:

options.text: Has significant demographic momentum and likely continued growth even with declining fertility

quiz_questions.question_text: The baby boom (1946–1964 in the US) appears on a pyramid as:

options.text: A bulge in the middle to upper age groups (now in their 60s–80s)

quiz_questions.question_text: Reading age structure diagrams for AP requires noting:

options.text: Shape (expansive/stationary/constrictive), dependency ratio, and demographic momentum implications

quiz_questions.question_text: China's one-child policy is visible in its pyramid as:

options.text: A narrowing in younger cohorts (smaller generations born after 1980)

quiz_questions.question_text: Aging populations face pandemic vulnerability because:

options.text: Young people are at risk only
Elderly are more susceptible to disease; higher healthcare demand (COVID-19 illustrated this)

quiz_questions.question_text: A country planning for education needs should examine:

options.text: Only the top of the pyramid
The base (ages 0–14) to project student enrollment and school infrastructure needs

quiz_questions.question_text: For retirement planning, analysts focus on:

options.text: The proportion approaching age 65+ compared to the working-age population (pension sustainability)

quiz_questions.question_text: Urbanization tends to _____ the base of the pyramid.

options.text: Narrow (urban populations have lower fertility rates than rural populations)

quiz_questions.question_text: Immigration can alter a host country's pyramid by:

options.text: Adding working-age adults (often widening the middle of the pyramid)

quiz_questions.question_text: Which data source is used to construct age structure diagrams?

options.text: Census data and demographic surveys (counting population by age and sex)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, you may be asked to:

options.text: Interpret a given pyramid, predict future growth, and recommend policy based on demographic data

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Carrying Capacity
summary_sections.content_html:
Carrying Capacity & Human Population

Earth's carrying capacity for humans is debated. Estimates range from 4 to 16 billion depending on consumption levels and technology.

Key Concepts

  • Ecological footprint: Area of productive land/water needed to sustain a person's lifestyle.
  • Overshoot: Humanity currently uses ~1.7 Earths' worth of resources per year.
  • Earth Overshoot Day: Date humanity has used more resources than Earth can regenerate that year (moves earlier each decade).

flashcards.definition: Total area of productive land and water required to support a person's resource consumption and waste.
Annual date when humanity has consumed more resources than Earth can regenerate in a year.
Earth's biological ability to regenerate resources and absorb waste in a given year.
When resource consumption exceeds biocapacity; humanity currently uses ~1.7 Earths of resources.
Average footprint per person; varies enormously by country (US ~8 gha vs. India ~1.2 gha).

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth's carrying capacity for humans is estimated between:

options.text: 4–16 billion (widely debated; depends on consumption and technology assumptions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecological footprint measures:

options.text: Total productive land and water needed to sustain a person's lifestyle and absorb waste

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth Overshoot Day occurs when:

options.text: Humanity has consumed more resources than Earth can regenerate that year

quiz_questions.question_text: In recent years, Earth Overshoot Day has fallen in approximately:

options.text: Late July to early August (meaning we use ~1.7 Earths/year)

quiz_questions.question_text: If everyone lived like the average American, we would need approximately:
If everyone lived like the average Indian, we would need:
Biocapacity is the ability of ecosystems to:

options.text: Regenerate resources and absorb waste (measured in global hectares)

quiz_questions.question_text: The US per capita ecological footprint is approximately:
Factors determining carrying capacity include:

options.text: Food, water, energy, waste absorption capacity, technology, and per-capita consumption

quiz_questions.question_text: Technology can increase carrying capacity by:

options.text: Improving agricultural yields, water efficiency, renewable energy, and resource recycling

quiz_questions.question_text: The Green Revolution increased food production through:

options.text: High-yield crop varieties, irrigation, fertilizers, and pesticides

quiz_questions.question_text: Critics of the Green Revolution note it:

options.text: Increased environmental degradation (soil depletion, water use, pesticide pollution, biodiversity loss)

quiz_questions.question_text: The IPAT model assesses human impact using:

options.text: I = P × A × T (Population × Affluence × Technology)

quiz_questions.question_text: Reducing ecological footprint can be achieved by:

options.text: Lower consumption, renewable energy, sustainable agriculture, reduced waste, and efficient technology

quiz_questions.question_text: Water scarcity affects approximately _____ of the world's population seasonally.
The concept of 'limits to growth' was popularized by a _____ report.

options.text: 1972 Club of Rome (Meadows et al.) 'The Limits to Growth' study

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecological debt occurs when a country's footprint exceeds its:

options.text: Biocapacity (it consumes more resources than its ecosystems can regenerate)

quiz_questions.question_text: Reducing food waste would help because roughly _____ of food produced is wasted globally.
Carrying capacity is not fixed because:

options.text: Technology, climate, resource depletion, and consumption patterns all change it over time

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should connect carrying capacity to:
