summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Urbanization & Land Use
summary_sections.content_html:
Urbanization & Land Use

Over 56% of the world's population lives in cities (projected 68% by 2050). Urbanization concentrates consumption but can also create efficiencies.

Impacts

  • Urban heat island: Cities are warmer than surroundings (concrete + less vegetation + waste heat).
  • Sprawl: Low-density, car-dependent expansion converts farmland/habitat.
  • Smart growth: Compact, mixed-use, transit-oriented development to reduce sprawl.

flashcards.definition: Urban areas being warmer than surrounding rural areas due to concrete, asphalt, reduced vegetation, and waste heat.
Low-density, car-dependent suburban expansion that converts farmland and natural habitat.
Urban planning strategy: compact, walkable, mixed-use, transit-oriented development to reduce sprawl.
Roads, rooftops, and pavement that prevent water infiltration → increased runoff and flooding.
Movement of people from rural to urban areas; >56% of the world's population now lives in cities.

quiz_questions.question_text: What percentage of the world's population currently lives in cities?
Urban heat island effect is caused by:

options.text: Dark surfaces (asphalt, concrete), reduced vegetation, waste heat from buildings/vehicles, and less evapotranspiration

quiz_questions.question_text: Urban sprawl is characterized by:
Environmental impacts of urban sprawl include:

options.text: Habitat loss, increased car emissions, higher energy use, loss of agricultural land, and increased runoff

quiz_questions.question_text: Smart growth principles include:

options.text: Compact development, mixed land use, public transit, walkability, and open space preservation

quiz_questions.question_text: Impervious surfaces increase:

options.text: Stormwater runoff, flooding, and non-point-source water pollution

quiz_questions.explanation: Water flows over, not into ground.

quiz_questions.question_text: Green infrastructure in cities includes:

options.text: Green roofs, permeable pavements, rain gardens, urban forests, and bioswales

quiz_questions.question_text: Urbanization can be beneficial because cities:

options.text: Can be more energy-efficient per capita — higher density reduces transportation needs and enables shared services

quiz_questions.question_text: Transportation accounts for approximately _____ of US greenhouse gas emissions.
Transit-oriented development centers housing and services around:
Agricultural land loss to urbanization threatens:

options.text: Food security and local food production (prime farmland is often near cities)

quiz_questions.question_text: Brownfields are:

options.text: Abandoned or underused industrial/commercial sites, often contaminated, that can be redeveloped

quiz_questions.question_text: Infill development (building on vacant urban lots) helps by:

options.text: Reducing sprawl by using existing urban land more efficiently

quiz_questions.question_text: Urban parks and green spaces provide:

options.text: Improved air quality, reduced heat island, mental health benefits, stormwater management, and biodiversity habitat

quiz_questions.question_text: Megacities (>10 million people) face challenges including:

options.text: Water supply, waste management, air quality, transportation, and housing affordability

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'walkability' promotes:

options.text: Mixed-use neighborhoods where daily needs are within walking distance, reducing emissions and improving health

quiz_questions.question_text: Land use zoning can reduce environmental impact by:

options.text: Separating incompatible uses, protecting open space, and directing growth to appropriate areas

quiz_questions.question_text: Heat-related illness in cities is worsened by:

options.text: Urban heat island effect, especially during heat waves (concrete retains heat; lack of shade)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sustainable urban planning for AP connects to:

options.text: Energy use, water management, air quality, biodiversity, transportation, and environmental justice

quiz_questions.question_text: Cities that successfully reduce their environmental footprint often:

options.text: Invest in public transit, green buildings, renewable energy, waste reduction, and urban green spaces

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Population Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Population Policy

China's One-Child Policy (1980–2015)

Reduced TFR from ~6 to ~1.7. Now facing aging crisis; shifted to two-child (2016) then three-child (2021) policy.

India

Voluntary family planning, women's education. TFR dropped from ~6 (1960) to ~2.0 (2023). Now world's most populous country.

Kerala Model

Indian state achieved low TFR (~1.6) through high female literacy (97%), good healthcare, without coercion.

flashcards.definition: 1980–2015 policy limiting most families to one child; reduced TFR but caused demographic imbalance.
Indian state that achieved low fertility (~1.6 TFR) through female education and healthcare, without coercion.
Government policies encouraging higher birth rates (e.g., France, Sweden, South Korea offering incentives).
Government policies discouraging births/reducing population growth (e.g., China's one-child policy).
Education, economic opportunities, and reproductive rights for women — the most effective way to reduce fertility.

quiz_questions.question_text: China's one-child policy was in effect from:

A major consequence of China's one-child policy was:

options.text: Aging population, gender imbalance (preference for boys), and a shrinking workforce

quiz_questions.question_text: China shifted to a three-child policy in _____ to address demographic challenges.
India's approach to population control has been primarily:

options.text: Voluntary family planning, women's education, and healthcare improvements

quiz_questions.question_text: India's TFR dropped from ~6 (1960) to approximately _____ by 2023.
The Kerala model demonstrates that low fertility can be achieved through:

options.text: High female literacy (~97%), good healthcare, and women's empowerment — without coercive measures

quiz_questions.question_text: Kerala's TFR is approximately:

options.text: ~1.6 (well below India's national average and below replacement)

quiz_questions.question_text: Pronatalist policies in France include:

options.text: Generous parental leave, childcare subsidies, tax benefits for families, and family allowances

quiz_questions.question_text: South Korea's TFR has dropped to ~0.7, which is:

options.text: One of the lowest in the world and far below replacement

quiz_questions.question_text: Singapore shifted from anti-natalist to pro-natalist policies because:

options.text: TFR dropped below replacement; concerns about aging workforce and economic sustainability

quiz_questions.question_text: The most effective way to reduce fertility rates globally is:

options.text: Educating and empowering women (girls' education, economic opportunities, reproductive choice)

quiz_questions.question_text: Unmet need for family planning means:

options.text: Women who want to delay or prevent pregnancy but lack access to contraception (~218 million women globally)

quiz_questions.question_text: Coercive population policies raise ethical concerns about:

options.text: Individual rights, reproductive freedom, gender equality, and human rights

quiz_questions.question_text: Iran's population policy shifted from:

options.text: Encouraging large families (1980s) to one of the most successful family planning programs, then back to pro-natalist

quiz_questions.question_text: Japan addresses population decline through:

options.text: Automation/robotics, modest pro-natalist incentives, and cautious immigration reform

quiz_questions.question_text: Population policies are most successful when they:

options.text: Are voluntary, culturally sensitive, include women's empowerment, and address economic factors

quiz_questions.question_text: The relationship between population policy and environment is:

options.text: Direct: lower population growth → less resource demand, pollution, and habitat loss → reduced environmental impact

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, population policy case studies test understanding of:

options.text: Demographic transitions, policy approaches (voluntary vs. coercive), social impacts, and environmental connections

quiz_questions.question_text: A lesson from all these case studies is that:

options.text: Context matters — effective population policy must account for culture, economics, education, and women's rights

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Demographic Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Demographic Calculations

Key Formulas

  • Growth rate (r): r = (CBR − CDR) / 10 (gives %; CBR and CDR per 1,000).
  • Doubling time: t = 70 / r (Rule of 70).
  • Rate of natural increase: (births − deaths) / total population × 100.
  • Net migration rate: (immigration − emigration) / population × 1,000.
  • Crude birth rate / Crude death rate: births or deaths per 1,000 people per year.

flashcards.definition: Number of live births per 1,000 people per year.
Number of deaths per 1,000 people per year.
(CBR − CDR) / 10 → expressed as a percentage. Does not include migration.
(Immigration − Emigration) per 1,000 people per year.
Number of infant deaths (<1 year old) per 1,000 live births; key indicator of development.

A country with CBR=12, CDR=11, and net migration=+3/1000 has total growth rate of:

options.text: Deaths of infants under age 1 per 1,000 live births

options.text: Low development, poor healthcare, and inadequate nutrition/sanitation

options.text: 50–65 years (lower due to disease, poverty, limited healthcare)

In the same country (500K births, 200K deaths, 25M pop), CDR is:

options.text: Average number of children per woman during her lifetime

quiz_questions.question_text: A TFR of 6 means on average each woman has:

options.text: People per unit area (e.g., per km²)

options.text: Arable (farmable) land area — a better indicator of population pressure on food production

quiz_questions.question_text: A population pyramid with equal-width bars at all ages predicts:
If a population of 1 million grows at 3% per year, next year it will be approximately:
For AP free-response questions on demographics, students should:

options.text: Show all calculations, label formulas, interpret results, and connect to environmental impacts

options.text: Multiple-choice and free-response questions across several AP Environmental Science units

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Renewable vs. Nonrenewable Resources
summary_sections.content_html:
Renewable vs. Nonrenewable Resources

Renewable: Replenished on a human timescale — solar, wind, water, biomass, geothermal. Sustainable if used at or below replacement rate.

Nonrenewable: Formed over millions of years — fossil fuels, minerals, metals. Finite; extraction degrades the environment.

Key Distinction

Even renewables can become depleted if overexploited (e.g., overfishing, deforestation faster than regrowth).

flashcards.definition: Resource replenished naturally on a human timescale (solar, wind, water, biomass).
Resource formed over geological time; finite supply (fossil fuels, minerals, metals).
Maximum amount of a renewable resource that can be harvested without depleting stock.
Using a resource faster than it can be replaced, leading to decline or exhaustion.
Resource that is virtually inexhaustible on any human timescale (e.g., solar energy, tidal energy).

quiz_questions.question_text: Renewable resources are those that:

options.text: Take millions of years to form
Are replenished naturally on a human timescale (e.g., solar, wind, biomass)

quiz_questions.question_text: Nonrenewable resources include:

options.text: Fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) and minerals/metals

quiz_questions.question_text: A perpetual resource like solar energy differs from renewable because it:

options.text: Is virtually inexhaustible (available as long as the sun shines, regardless of human use)

quiz_questions.question_text: A renewable resource can become depleted if:

options.text: It's harvested faster than it regenerates (e.g., overfishing, deforestation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sustainable yield is the:

options.text: Largest harvest that can be taken indefinitely without reducing the resource base

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is nonrenewable?

options.text: Coal (formed over millions of years from ancient plant matter)

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is considered renewable?
Fossil fuels formed from:

options.text: Ancient organic matter compressed and heated over millions of years

quiz_questions.question_text: Peak oil theory suggests:

options.text: Oil production will reach a maximum and then decline as reserves are depleted

quiz_questions.question_text: The tragedy of the commons describes:

options.text: Overexploitation of shared resources when individuals act in self-interest (Garrett Hardin, 1968)

quiz_questions.question_text: Solutions to the tragedy of the commons include:

options.text: Regulation, privatization, community management, and international agreements

quiz_questions.question_text: Minerals and metals are nonrenewable but can be extended through:

options.text: Recycling, reuse, substitution, and improved extraction efficiency

quiz_questions.question_text: Rare earth elements are critical for:

options.text: Electronics, renewable energy tech (wind turbines, EV batteries), and defense systems

quiz_questions.question_text: Phosphorus is a critical nonrenewable resource for:

options.text: Agriculture (essential fertilizer nutrient); reserves are concentrated in few countries

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'resource curse' describes how:

options.text: Countries rich in natural resources often struggle with corruption, conflict, and poor economic outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: Substitution can help with resource depletion by:

options.text: Replacing a scarce resource with an abundant alternative (e.g., fiber optics replacing copper wire)

quiz_questions.question_text: Lifecycle analysis (LCA) evaluates:

options.text: Environmental impact of a product from extraction → manufacturing → use → disposal (cradle to grave)

quiz_questions.question_text: The circular economy model aims to:

options.text: Eliminate waste by designing products for reuse, repair, and recycling (closed-loop system)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding resource types is essential for AP because it connects to:

options.text: Energy policy, pollution, land use, sustainability, economic development, and environmental degradation

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should be able to:

options.text: Compare renewable vs. nonrenewable, evaluate sustainability, and propose management strategies

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Water Resources & Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Water Resources & Management

Only ~2.5% of Earth's water is fresh; ~69% of that is in glaciers/ice caps. Available fresh water (<1%) comes from groundwater, rivers, and lakes.

Key Issues

  • Aquifer depletion: Ogallala Aquifer depleting faster than recharge (irrigation in Great Plains).
  • Water pollution: Point source (factory pipe) vs. Non-point source (agricultural runoff).
  • Water stress: ~4 billion people face severe water scarcity at least one month per year.

flashcards.definition: Underground layer of permeable rock or sediment that holds and transmits groundwater.
Massive US aquifer underlying the Great Plains; being depleted by irrigation faster than it recharges.
Pollution from a single identifiable source (e.g., factory pipe, sewage outfall).
Pollution from diffuse sources (e.g., agricultural runoff, urban stormwater).
Upper surface of the zone of saturation in an aquifer; drops when pumping exceeds recharge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What percentage of Earth's water is fresh?
Of all fresh water, approximately _____ is locked in glaciers and ice caps.
Available fresh water for human use (rivers, lakes, accessible groundwater) is:
An aquifer is:

options.text: An underground layer of rock/sediment that holds and transmits groundwater

quiz_questions.question_text: The Ogallala Aquifer is being depleted because:

options.text: Irrigation withdrawals far exceed the very slow natural recharge rate

quiz_questions.question_text: Point source pollution comes from:

options.text: A single, identifiable location (e.g., a factory discharge pipe or sewage outfall)

quiz_questions.question_text: Non-point source pollution comes from:

options.text: Diffuse sources spread over a large area (e.g., agricultural runoff, urban stormwater, atmospheric deposition)

quiz_questions.question_text: The leading cause of water pollution in the US is:

options.text: Agricultural runoff (fertilizers, pesticides, animal waste — the largest non-point source)

quiz_questions.question_text: Eutrophication occurs when excess _____ enters waterways.

options.text: Nutrients (nitrogen and phosphorus) causing algal blooms → oxygen depletion

quiz_questions.question_text: A dead zone is an area of water with:

options.text: Very low dissolved oxygen (hypoxia) — organisms can't survive

quiz_questions.question_text: Water table depletion can cause:

options.text: Land subsidence (sinking), saltwater intrusion in coastal areas, and dried-up wells

quiz_questions.question_text: Desalination converts saltwater to freshwater using:
Limitations of desalination include:

options.text: High energy cost, brine waste disposal, and expense — not economically feasible everywhere

quiz_questions.question_text: Water conservation strategies include:

options.text: Drip irrigation, low-flow fixtures, water recycling, xeriscaping, and fixing leaks

quiz_questions.question_text: Drip irrigation is more efficient than flood irrigation because it:

options.text: Delivers water directly to plant roots, reducing evaporation and runoff (up to 95% efficient vs. 60%)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dams provide benefits including:

options.text: Water storage, hydropower, flood control, and irrigation — but also displace communities and alter ecosystems

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Water Act (1972, US) regulates:

options.text: Discharge of pollutants into surface waters and sets quality standards for surface waters

quiz_questions.question_text: The Safe Drinking Water Act regulates:

options.text: Public drinking water quality standards and groundwater protection

quiz_questions.question_text: Watershed management takes a _____ approach to water quality.

options.text: Holistic (managing the entire drainage area that feeds a water body)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, water resource questions often involve:

options.text: Identifying pollution sources (point vs. non-point), evaluating management strategies, and water budget calculations

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Soil & Agriculture
summary_sections.content_html:
Soil & Agriculture

Soil forms from weathered rock + organic matter over centuries. Soil horizons: O (organic litter), A (topsoil), B (subsoil), C (weathered parent material), R (bedrock).

Agricultural Impacts

  • Erosion: Wind and water remove topsoil; ~24 billion tons lost annually.
  • Salinization: Salt buildup from irrigation in arid regions.
  • Green Revolution: High-yield crops + fertilizers + irrigation; fed billions but increased environmental costs.

flashcards.definition: Layers of soil: O (organic), A (topsoil), B (subsoil), C (weathered parent material), R (bedrock).
Uppermost mineral layer; richest in organic matter and nutrients; essential for agriculture.
Removal of topsoil by wind and water; accelerated by deforestation, overgrazing, and poor farming practices.
Buildup of salts in soil from evaporation of irrigation water; reduces soil fertility.
1960s–70s agricultural transformation using high-yield varieties, fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation.

quiz_questions.question_text: The correct order of soil horizons from top to bottom is:

options.text: O, A, B, C, R (organic, topsoil, subsoil, parent material, bedrock)

quiz_questions.question_text: Topsoil (A horizon) is critical because it:

options.text: Contains the most organic matter, nutrients, and organisms — essential for plant growth

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately _____ billion tons of topsoil are lost to erosion annually.
Soil erosion is accelerated by:

options.text: Deforestation, overgrazing, poor farming practices, and leaving soil bare

quiz_questions.question_text: Salinization occurs when:

options.text: Irrigation water evaporates in arid regions, leaving dissolved salts behind in the soil

quiz_questions.question_text: The Green Revolution increased food production through:

options.text: High-yield crop varieties, synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation

quiz_questions.question_text: Negative impacts of the Green Revolution include:

options.text: Soil degradation, water pollution from fertilizers/pesticides, aquifer depletion, biodiversity loss, and fossil fuel dependence

quiz_questions.question_text: No-till agriculture reduces erosion by:

options.text: Not disturbing soil between plantings — crop residue protects against wind and water erosion

quiz_questions.question_text: Contour plowing involves:

options.text: Plowing along the contour (same elevation) of a slope to reduce water runoff and erosion

quiz_questions.question_text: Terracing creates stepped fields on hillsides to:

options.text: Reduce water runoff speed and soil erosion on steep slopes

quiz_questions.question_text: Cover crops protect soil by:

options.text: Covering bare soil between planting seasons to prevent erosion, add nutrients, and improve soil structure

quiz_questions.question_text: Crop rotation benefits soil by:

options.text: Alternating crops to prevent nutrient depletion, break pest cycles, and improve soil health

quiz_questions.question_text: Monoculture (planting one crop) increases vulnerability to:

options.text: Pests, diseases, and soil nutrient depletion (no diversity to buffer problems)

quiz_questions.question_text: Organic farming avoids:

options.text: Synthetic pesticides and fertilizers — relies on natural pest control, composting, and crop rotation

quiz_questions.question_text: Desertification is the process of:

options.text: Productive land becoming desert-like due to overgrazing, deforestation, drought, and poor farming practices

quiz_questions.question_text: The Dust Bowl (1930s, US Great Plains) was caused by:

options.text: A combination of poor farming practices (removing native grasses) and severe drought → massive topsoil loss

quiz_questions.question_text: Soil compaction from heavy machinery reduces:

options.text: Pore space, water infiltration, root growth, and aeration — harming soil productivity

quiz_questions.question_text: Soil pH affects plant growth because it determines:

options.text: Nutrient availability (most crops prefer pH 6.0–7.0; acidic or alkaline soils lock up nutrients)

quiz_questions.question_text: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) combines:

options.text: Biological control, crop rotation, resistant varieties, and minimal pesticide use

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should connect soil to:

options.text: Agriculture, erosion, water quality, food security, and sustainability (soil is the foundation of terrestrial ecosystems)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Forests & Deforestation
summary_sections.content_html:
Forests & Deforestation

Forests cover ~31% of Earth's land. They provide ecosystem services: carbon storage, biodiversity habitat, water cycling, soil protection, timber, and recreation.

Deforestation

~10 million hectares/year lost (2015–2020). Main drivers: cattle ranching, soy/palm oil farming, logging, and urbanization. Tropical forests are most threatened.

flashcards.definition: Permanent clearing of forests for other land uses (agriculture, ranching, urban development).
Forests absorb more CO₂ than they release; loss converts them to carbon sources.
Mature forest that has not been significantly disturbed; highest biodiversity and carbon storage.
Replanting trees on previously forested land.
Managing forests to meet current timber needs without compromising future forest health or biodiversity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Forests cover approximately _____ of Earth's land surface.
The leading cause of tropical deforestation is:

options.text: Agriculture (cattle ranching, soy, and palm oil plantations)

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately _____ hectares of forest are lost per year (2015–2020).

options.text: ~10 million (roughly the size of Iceland annually)

quiz_questions.question_text: Forests are carbon sinks because they:

options.text: Absorb more CO₂ through photosynthesis than they release through respiration

quiz_questions.question_text: Deforestation converts forests from carbon sinks to:

options.text: Carbon sources (stored carbon is released as CO₂ when trees are burned or decompose)

quiz_questions.question_text: Old-growth forests are ecologically valuable because they:

options.text: Have the highest biodiversity, most carbon storage, and complex ecosystem structures developed over centuries

quiz_questions.question_text: The Amazon rainforest produces approximately _____ of Earth's oxygen.

options.text: ~6% (though its role in carbon cycling is much larger)

quiz_questions.explanation: Often overstated; CO₂ absorption is key.

quiz_questions.question_text: Slash-and-burn agriculture involves:

options.text: Cutting and burning forest to clear land for farming; nutrients are quickly depleted from tropical soils

quiz_questions.question_text: Tropical soils are often nutrient-poor because:

options.text: Most nutrients are in the biomass (living organisms), not the soil; rapid decomposition and leaching

quiz_questions.question_text: Palm oil production is linked to deforestation in:

options.text: Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Malaysia) and increasingly in tropical regions worldwide

quiz_questions.question_text: Reforestation means:

options.text: Replanting trees on previously forested land

quiz_questions.question_text: Afforestation means:

options.text: Planting trees on land that was not previously forested

quiz_questions.question_text: Selective logging is less destructive than clear-cutting because it:

options.text: Removes only targeted trees, maintaining forest structure and biodiversity
Removes the most valuable trees only

quiz_questions.question_text: Clear-cutting removes all trees in an area, causing:

options.text: Erosion, habitat loss, sedimentation of streams, and loss of ecosystem services

quiz_questions.question_text: Certification programs like FSC (Forest Stewardship Council) promote:

options.text: Sustainable forestry practices verified by independent audits

quiz_questions.question_text: REDD+ is an international program that:

options.text: Provides financial incentives to developing countries for Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and forest Degradation

quiz_questions.question_text: Deforestation contributes approximately _____ of global CO₂ emissions.
Mangrove forests are particularly important because they:

options.text: Protect coastlines, serve as fish nurseries, and store carbon at 3-5× the rate of terrestrial forests

quiz_questions.question_text: Urban deforestation contributes to:

options.text: Heat island effects, increased flooding, reduced air quality, and loss of urban biodiversity

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, deforestation connects to:

options.text: Climate change, biodiversity loss, soil erosion, water cycling, and indigenous rights — a multi-dimensional issue

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Fisheries & Ocean Resources
summary_sections.content_html:
Fisheries & Ocean Resources

Fish provide ~17% of animal protein consumed globally. ~34% of fish stocks are overfished; ~60% are fished at maximum sustained yield.

Key Concepts

  • Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY): Largest catch that can be taken indefinitely.
  • Bycatch: Unintended species caught and often discarded (dolphins, turtles, sharks).
  • Aquaculture: Farmed fish; now supplies >50% of fish consumed but has environmental impacts.

flashcards.definition: Largest harvest that can be taken from a fish stock indefinitely without depleting it.
Harvesting fish faster than they can reproduce, leading to population decline.
Unintended capture of non-target species (dolphins, sea turtles, sharks) in fishing operations.
Farming of fish, shellfish, or aquatic plants; provides >50% of fish consumed globally.
Destructive fishing method dragging heavy nets along the ocean floor, damaging seafloor habitats.

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately _____ of global fish stocks are overfished.
Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) is:

options.text: The largest catch that can be taken indefinitely without depleting the stock

quiz_questions.question_text: Bycatch includes:

options.text: Unintended species caught during fishing operations (dolphins, sea turtles, juvenile fish, sharks)

quiz_questions.question_text: Aquaculture now provides _____ of fish consumed globally.
Environmental impacts of aquaculture include:

options.text: Water pollution (waste, antibiotics, chemicals), habitat destruction (mangrove clearing), disease spread to wild fish, and escape of non-native species

quiz_questions.question_text: Bottom trawling is destructive because it:

options.text: Drags heavy nets across the seafloor, destroying coral, sponges, and benthic habitats — like bulldozing a forest to catch deer

quiz_questions.question_text: Fishing down the food web means:

options.text: Progressively targeting smaller, lower-trophic-level species as larger predatory fish are depleted

quiz_questions.question_text: Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs) help manage fisheries by:

options.text: Giving fishers tradable shares of the total allowable catch — economic incentive to conserve

quiz_questions.question_text: Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) help fisheries by:

options.text: Providing refuges where fish can reproduce and grow, replenishing surrounding fishing areas (spillover effect)

quiz_questions.question_text: The collapse of Atlantic cod fisheries off Newfoundland (1992) demonstrated:

options.text: That even abundant species can collapse from overfishing — the moratorium put 40,000 out of work; stocks still haven't fully recovered

quiz_questions.question_text: Ghost fishing occurs when:

options.text: Abandoned or lost fishing gear (nets, traps) continues to catch and kill marine organisms

quiz_questions.question_text: Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) fishing accounts for up to:

options.text: ~30% of global catch in some fisheries

quiz_questions.question_text: Shark finning (removing fins and discarding the body) has caused:

options.text: Dramatic declines in shark populations worldwide (~71% decline in oceanic sharks since 1970)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sustainable fishing practices include:

options.text: Catch limits, gear restrictions (e.g., turtle excluder devices), seasonal closures, and size limits

quiz_questions.question_text: Pole-and-line fishing is more sustainable than trawling because it:

options.text: Is highly selective (targets specific species, minimal bycatch)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecosystem-based fisheries management considers:

options.text: The entire marine ecosystem including food web interactions, habitat, and non-target species

quiz_questions.question_text: Subsidies that encourage overfishing should be:

options.text: Reformed or eliminated (many governments subsidize fuel, gear, and boat building that enable overcapacity)

quiz_questions.question_text: Coral reef destruction threatens fisheries because reefs:

options.text: Support ~25% of all marine species and serve as nursery habitat for many commercially important fish

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, fisheries management connects to:

options.text: Population dynamics, carrying capacity, tragedy of the commons, international policy, and marine ecology

quiz_questions.question_text: The precautionary approach to fisheries means:

options.text: Setting conservative catch limits when scientific uncertainty exists — erring on the side of caution

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Resource Use
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Resource Use

Aral Sea Disaster

Soviet irrigation diverted rivers feeding the Aral Sea → lost 90% of volume by 2010. Desertification, salt storms, fishery collapse, health crises. Partial recovery in North Aral with dam.

Three Gorges Dam (China)

World's largest hydroelectric dam. Generates ~100 TWh/year. But: displaced 1.3 million people, causes erosion, sedimentation, and reduced biodiversity downstream.

flashcards.definition: Soviet-era irrigation diversions caused one of the worst environmental disasters; Aral Sea lost ~90% volume.
World's largest hydroelectric dam on China's Yangtze River; enormous power but major social and environmental costs.
US Great Plains aquifer being drained for irrigation faster than recharge; threatens food production.
Minerals mined in conflict zones (coltan, tin, tungsten, gold) whose trade funds armed groups.
Paradox where countries rich in natural resources often have poor economic outcomes and governance.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Aral Sea shrank primarily because:

options.text: Soviet irrigation projects diverted the two rivers (Amu Darya and Syr Darya) that fed it

quiz_questions.question_text: The Aral Sea lost approximately _____ of its volume by 2010.
Consequences of the Aral Sea disaster include:

options.text: Desertification, toxic salt/dust storms, fishery collapse, health crises, and economic devastation

quiz_questions.question_text: The North Aral Sea has partially recovered due to:

options.text: Construction of the Kok-Aral Dam (2005) by Kazakhstan, which retained water in the northern section

quiz_questions.question_text: Three Gorges Dam generates approximately _____ TWh of electricity per year.

options.text: ~100 (making it the world's largest power station by installed capacity)

quiz_questions.question_text: Three Gorges Dam displaced approximately:
Environmental impacts of Three Gorges Dam include:

options.text: Downstream erosion, sediment trapping, reduced biodiversity, landslide risk, and water quality changes

quiz_questions.question_text: The Ogallala Aquifer is critical because it:

options.text: Provides ~30% of US irrigation water; supports billions of dollars in agriculture in the Great Plains

quiz_questions.question_text: The Ogallala Aquifer is being depleted because:

options.text: Pumping rates far exceed the very slow natural recharge (~0.5-2.5 cm/year)

quiz_questions.question_text: Coltan mining in the Democratic Republic of Congo is linked to:

options.text: Armed conflict, human rights abuses, environmental destruction, and funding of militia groups

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'resource curse' applies to countries like:

options.text: Nigeria (oil), DRC (minerals), Venezuela (oil) — resource wealth + poor governance = conflict and poverty

quiz_questions.question_text: Chile's Atacama Desert is important for mining:

options.text: Lithium and copper (critical for batteries, electronics, and renewable energy technology)

quiz_questions.question_text: Mining environmental impacts include:

options.text: Habitat destruction, water contamination (acid mine drainage), air pollution, soil degradation, and waste piles

quiz_questions.question_text: Acid mine drainage occurs when:

options.text: Exposed sulfide minerals in mine waste react with water and air to produce sulfuric acid, contaminating waterways

quiz_questions.question_text: The Deepwater Horizon oil spill (2010) released:

options.text: ~4.9 million barrels of oil into the Gulf of Mexico (largest marine oil spill in US history)

quiz_questions.question_text: These case studies share common themes of:

options.text: Trade-offs between resource use and environmental/social costs; unintended consequences of large-scale projects

quiz_questions.question_text: Lessons from these cases suggest that resource management needs:

options.text: Environmental impact assessments, long-term thinking, stakeholder input, and precautionary approaches

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) are important because they:

options.text: Evaluate potential environmental consequences before a project begins, allowing informed decision-making

quiz_questions.question_text: International cooperation is needed for resource management because:

options.text: Many resources cross borders (water, fish, atmosphere), and global trade connects distant impacts

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, case studies test the ability to:

options.text: Analyze complex trade-offs, identify stakeholders, evaluate policies, and propose sustainable alternatives

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Resource Management Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Resource Management Models

Key Models & Concepts

  • Tragedy of the commons: Shared resources depleted by self-interest (Hardin, 1968). Solutions: regulation, privatization, community management.
  • Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY): Largest harvest sustainable indefinitely. Applied to fisheries, forestry.
  • Cost-benefit analysis: Weighing economic costs vs. benefits of a resource decision; externalities often excluded.
  • Precautionary principle: When uncertain, err on the side of protection.

flashcards.definition: Shared resources depleted when individuals act in self-interest; solutions include regulation, privatization, and community management.
Economic tool comparing costs and benefits of a project or policy; often fails to account for environmental externalities.
A cost or benefit not reflected in market prices (e.g., pollution costs borne by society, not the polluter).
When potential for harm exists but science is uncertain, take preventive action to avoid environmental damage.
Flexible resource management approach that adjusts strategies based on monitoring and new data.

quiz_questions.question_text: The tragedy of the commons describes:

options.text: Overuse of shared resources when individuals maximize personal gain at collective expense

quiz_questions.question_text: Garrett Hardin proposed solutions including:
Elinor Ostrom's research showed that:

options.text: Communities can successfully self-manage common resources through local rules and cooperation (Nobel Prize 2009)

quiz_questions.question_text: MSY assumes population growth follows:

options.text: Logistic growth — maximum reproduction occurs at N = K/2

quiz_questions.question_text: Cost-benefit analysis often underestimates environmental damage because:

options.text: Externalities (pollution costs, ecosystem service losses) are difficult to monetize and often excluded

quiz_questions.question_text: An externality is:

options.text: A cost or benefit not reflected in market prices (e.g., factory pollution harming neighbors)

quiz_questions.question_text: The precautionary principle states:

options.text: Take protective action when there's potential for serious harm, even without full scientific certainty

quiz_questions.question_text: Adaptive management involves:

options.text: Monitoring outcomes and adjusting management strategies based on new data and results

quiz_questions.question_text: A discount rate in resource economics:

options.text: Reduces the present value of future costs and benefits — high discount rates favor short-term exploitation

quiz_questions.question_text: High discount rates lead to:

options.text: More resource exploitation now (future resources are valued less than current ones)

quiz_questions.question_text: Natural capital refers to:

options.text: The stock of natural resources and ecosystem services that provide value to humans

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecosystem services include:

options.text: Provisioning (food, water), regulating (climate, flood control), supporting (nutrient cycles), and cultural (recreation, aesthetics)

quiz_questions.question_text: Payments for Ecosystem Services (PES) programs:

options.text: Compensate landowners for maintaining ecosystem services (e.g., paying farmers to protect watersheds)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cap-and-trade systems for managing resources:

options.text: Set an overall cap on resource use/pollution and allow trading of permits — market finds efficient allocation

quiz_questions.question_text: Subsidies can be harmful when they:

options.text: Encourage overexploitation of resources (e.g., fossil fuel subsidies, fishing subsidies that enable overcapacity)

quiz_questions.question_text: The safe minimum standard approach says we should:

options.text: Maintain the minimum viable population/resource level unless the costs of doing so are unacceptably high

quiz_questions.question_text: Intergenerational equity in resource management means:

options.text: Ensuring future generations have access to resources and a healthy environment (sustainability principle)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'triple bottom line' evaluates projects on:

options.text: Environmental, social, and economic outcomes (planet, people, profit)

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP free-response on resource management, students should:

options.text: Compare models (tragedy of commons, MSY, CBA), evaluate trade-offs, and apply the precautionary principle

quiz_questions.question_text: Effective resource management requires integrating:

options.text: Ecological science, economics, social equity, technology, and policy — no single approach is sufficient

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Fossil Fuels
summary_sections.content_html:
Fossil Fuels

Coal, oil (petroleum), and natural gas supply ~80% of global energy. Formed from ancient organic matter over millions of years.

Impacts

  • CO₂ emissions: Leading cause of climate change.
  • Air pollution: SO₂, NOₓ, particulates → smog, acid rain, respiratory illness.
  • Extraction damage: Mountaintop removal, oil spills, fracking (hydrofracturing) → water contamination.

Coal is dirtiest per unit energy; natural gas cleanest fossil fuel (~50% less CO₂ than coal). Oil used mostly for transportation.

flashcards.definition: Coal, oil, and natural gas; nonrenewable energy sources formed from ancient organic matter over millions of years.
Solid fossil fuel; dirtiest per unit energy; used mainly for electricity generation; produces more CO₂, SO₂, and particulates.
Cleanest-burning fossil fuel (~50% less CO₂ than coal); primarily methane (CH₄); growing share of energy.
Injecting high-pressure fluid into rock to fracture it and release oil/gas; concerns about water contamination and earthquakes.
Precipitation with pH < 5.6 caused by SO₂ and NOₓ from fossil fuel combustion reacting with atmospheric water.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fossil fuels currently supply approximately _____ of global energy.
Coal is the _____ fossil fuel in terms of CO₂ emissions per unit of energy.

options.text: Dirtiest (highest CO₂, SO₂, and particulate emissions per unit energy)

quiz_questions.question_text: Natural gas produces approximately _____ less CO₂ than coal per unit energy.
Oil (petroleum) is primarily used for:

options.text: Transportation (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel; ~90% of transport fuel)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fracking involves:

options.text: Injecting high-pressure fluid underground to fracture rock and release trapped oil and gas

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental concerns about fracking include:

options.text: Water contamination (chemicals in fracking fluid), induced seismicity (earthquakes), methane leakage, and water use

quiz_questions.question_text: Mountaintop removal mining for coal:

options.text: Blasts off mountain peaks to access coal seams, destroying ecosystems, filling valleys with debris

quiz_questions.question_text: SO₂ and NOₓ from fossil fuel combustion cause:

options.text: Acid rain, smog, respiratory disease, and damage to ecosystems and buildings

quiz_questions.question_text: Acid rain has a pH below:

options.text: 5.6 (normal rain is ~5.6 due to dissolved CO₂; acid rain is more acidic)

quiz_questions.question_text: Particulate matter (PM2.5) from fossil fuels causes:

options.text: Respiratory and cardiovascular disease (particles small enough to enter lungs and bloodstream)

quiz_questions.question_text: The greenhouse effect is enhanced by fossil fuel CO₂ because:

options.text: CO₂ traps infrared radiation (heat) re-emitted from Earth's surface, warming the atmosphere

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon capture and storage (CCS) aims to:

options.text: Capture CO₂ from power plants and store it underground, preventing atmospheric release

quiz_questions.question_text: Limitations of CCS include:

options.text: High cost, energy penalty (reduces plant efficiency), limited storage capacity, and potential leakage

quiz_questions.question_text: Methane (CH₄) from natural gas is a potent greenhouse gas because it:

options.text: Traps ~80× more heat than CO₂ over 20 years (though shorter-lived)

quiz_questions.question_text: Oil spills devastate marine ecosystems by:

options.text: Coating organisms, poisoning food chains, destroying habitats (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, Exxon Valdez)

quiz_questions.question_text: Coal ash (fly ash) contains:

options.text: Heavy metals (arsenic, mercury, lead) that can contaminate water and soil if improperly stored

quiz_questions.question_text: Mercury emissions from coal plants are dangerous because mercury:

options.text: Bioaccumulates and biomagnifies in food chains, causing neurological damage (especially in fish → humans)

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'stranded assets' refers to:

options.text: Fossil fuel reserves that may never be extracted if climate policies limit emissions (financially devalued)

quiz_questions.question_text: Transitioning from fossil fuels is challenging because:

options.text: Fossil fuels are deeply embedded in infrastructure, economies, and political systems (energy inertia)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should evaluate fossil fuels in terms of:

options.text: Environmental impacts (air, water, climate), extraction methods, alternatives, and policy options

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nuclear Energy
summary_sections.content_html:
Nuclear Energy

Fission: Splitting heavy atoms (uranium-235, plutonium-239) releases enormous energy. Provides ~10% of global electricity with near-zero CO₂ during operation.

Pros & Cons

  • Pros: High energy density, low carbon, reliable baseload power.
  • Cons: Radioactive waste (remains hazardous for thousands of years), meltdown risk (Chernobyl, Fukushima), high construction costs, uranium mining impacts.

flashcards.definition: Splitting heavy nuclei (U-235, Pu-239) to release energy; used in current nuclear power plants.
Spent nuclear fuel and contaminated materials; high-level waste remains hazardous for thousands of years.
Time for half of a radioactive substance to decay; Pu-239 has a half-life of ~24,000 years.
Uncontrolled reactor core overheating; e.g., Chernobyl (1986), Fukushima (2011).
Combining light nuclei (hydrogen) to release energy; powers the sun but not yet commercially viable on Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Nuclear fission involves:

options.text: Splitting heavy atoms (uranium-235) to release enormous energy

quiz_questions.question_text: Nuclear power provides approximately _____ of global electricity.
CO₂ emissions from nuclear power during operation are:

options.text: Near zero (no fossil fuel combustion during electricity generation)

quiz_questions.question_text: The main challenge of nuclear waste is:

options.text: It remains hazardous for thousands to hundreds of thousands of years (no permanent disposal solution exists)

quiz_questions.question_text: Half-life of plutonium-239 is approximately:
The Chernobyl disaster (1986) was caused by:

options.text: A flawed reactor design and operator error during a safety test, leading to a steam explosion and fire

quiz_questions.question_text: The Fukushima disaster (2011) was triggered by:

options.text: A massive earthquake and tsunami that knocked out cooling systems, causing meltdowns in three reactors

quiz_questions.question_text: Uranium mining can cause:

options.text: Habitat destruction, water contamination, radioactive tailings, and health risks for miners

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy density of nuclear fuel compared to fossil fuels is:

options.text: Much higher (one kg of uranium has ~2 million times the energy of one kg of coal)

quiz_questions.question_text: Nuclear power plants provide _____ power.
Spent nuclear fuel is currently stored primarily in:

options.text: On-site cooling pools and dry cask storage at reactor sites (no permanent geological repository operating in US)

quiz_questions.question_text: Yucca Mountain (Nevada) was proposed as a:

options.text: Permanent geological repository for US high-level nuclear waste (project stalled politically)

quiz_questions.question_text: Nuclear proliferation risk refers to:

options.text: The potential for nuclear technology/materials to be diverted for weapons production

quiz_questions.question_text: Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) are being developed to:

options.text: Reduce construction costs and time, improve safety through passive systems, and provide flexible deployment

quiz_questions.question_text: Nuclear fusion, if achieved, would provide:

options.text: Virtually unlimited energy with minimal waste (fuel is hydrogen isotopes; byproduct is helium)

quiz_questions.question_text: France generates approximately _____ of its electricity from nuclear power.

options.text: ~70% (one of the highest proportions in the world)

quiz_questions.question_text: The capacity factor of nuclear plants is typically:

options.text: High (~90%+ — among the most reliable energy sources)

quiz_questions.question_text: Thermal pollution from nuclear (and fossil) plants occurs when:

options.text: Heated cooling water is discharged into waterways, raising water temperature and reducing dissolved oxygen

quiz_questions.question_text: The debate over nuclear energy centers on:

options.text: Balancing its low-carbon benefits against waste, safety, cost, and proliferation risks

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should be able to:

options.text: Compare nuclear with fossil fuels and renewables, analyze risks, and evaluate its role in climate solutions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Renewable Energy (Solar, Wind, Hydro, Geothermal)
summary_sections.content_html:
Renewable Energy Sources

Solar

Photovoltaic (PV) cells convert sunlight directly to electricity. Fastest-growing energy source; costs dropped ~90% since 2010.

Wind

Turbines convert kinetic energy of wind. 2nd-fastest growing; concerns include bird/bat mortality and visual impact.

Hydroelectric

Largest renewable source (~16% of global electricity). Dams displace communities and alter ecosystems.

Geothermal

Heat from Earth's interior. Limited to tectonically active areas but very reliable.

flashcards.definition: Semiconductor device that converts sunlight directly into electricity.
Device converting kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy.
Electricity generated by flowing water through turbines in dams; largest renewable source globally.
Heat energy from Earth's interior used for electricity generation or direct heating.
Challenge of solar and wind: output varies with weather and time of day; requires storage or backup.

quiz_questions.question_text: The fastest-growing energy source globally is:

options.text: Solar (costs dropped ~90% since 2010; exponential deployment growth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Solar PV cells convert sunlight to electricity through:

options.text: The photovoltaic effect (photons excite electrons in semiconductor materials)

quiz_questions.question_text: Wind energy's main environmental concerns include:

options.text: Bird and bat mortality, visual/noise impact, and land use (though impacts are much less than fossil fuels)

quiz_questions.question_text: Hydroelectric power is the _____ source of renewable electricity globally.
Negative impacts of large hydroelectric dams include:

options.text: Displacing communities, blocking fish migration, flooding habitats, altering sediment flow, and methane from reservoirs

quiz_questions.question_text: Geothermal energy is most available in:

options.text: Tectonically active areas (plate boundaries, volcanic regions — Iceland, western US, East Africa)

quiz_questions.question_text: Advantages of geothermal energy include:

options.text: Very reliable (24/7), small footprint, near-zero emissions, and not intermittent

quiz_questions.question_text: Solar and wind are called intermittent because:

options.text: Output varies with weather, time of day, and season — they require storage or backup

quiz_questions.question_text: Solutions to intermittency include:

options.text: Battery storage, pumped hydro, grid interconnection, demand response, and overbuilding capacity

quiz_questions.question_text: Battery storage costs have _____ in the last decade.

options.text: Dropped dramatically (~90% decline for lithium-ion batteries)

quiz_questions.question_text: Net metering allows homeowners with solar panels to:

options.text: Sell excess electricity back to the grid, offsetting their electricity costs

quiz_questions.question_text: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) differs from PV because it:

options.text: Uses mirrors to focus sunlight to heat fluid → steam → turbine (can include thermal storage)

quiz_questions.question_text: Offshore wind farms offer advantages of:

options.text: Stronger, more consistent winds and reduced visual/noise impact on communities

quiz_questions.question_text: Run-of-river hydro is more environmentally friendly than dams because it:

options.text: Doesn't require a large reservoir; diverts part of the river flow through turbines with minimal ecological disruption

quiz_questions.question_text: The levelized cost of energy (LCOE) for solar and wind is now:

options.text: Competitive with or cheaper than new fossil fuel plants in most markets worldwide

quiz_questions.question_text: Solar energy limitations include:

options.text: Intermittency, land use for large-scale farms, manufacturing impacts (energy and materials), and recycling panels

quiz_questions.question_text: A smart grid helps integrate renewables by:

options.text: Dynamically managing electricity supply and demand using digital technology, improving efficiency and reliability

quiz_questions.question_text: The energy payback time for solar panels (time to generate as much energy as it took to make them) is:

options.text: ~1-3 years (then they produce clean energy for 25-30+ years)

quiz_questions.question_text: Hybrid systems combine multiple renewable sources to:

options.text: Improve reliability (e.g., solar + wind + storage; when one is low, others may be producing)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should compare renewable sources by:

options.text: Analyzing energy output, environmental impacts, costs, reliability, geographic constraints, and scalability

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Energy Efficiency & Conservation
summary_sections.content_html:
Energy Efficiency & Conservation

Efficiency: Getting more useful work from less energy input. Conservation: Using less energy through behavioral changes.

Key Strategies

  • LED lighting uses ~75% less energy than incandescent.
  • Building insulation, efficient appliances, ENERGY STAR standards.
  • Cogeneration (CHP): Uses waste heat from electricity generation for heating (~80% efficiency vs. ~33% for conventional plants).
  • Transportation: EVs, public transit, fuel economy standards.

flashcards.definition: Getting more useful output from less energy input (e.g., LED vs. incandescent bulbs).
Reducing energy use through behavioral changes (e.g., turning off lights, lowering thermostat).
Combined Heat and Power — using waste heat from electricity production for heating; ~80% total efficiency.
US EPA/DOE program certifying energy-efficient appliances and buildings.
A unit of energy saved through efficiency; often cheaper than generating new energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy efficiency means:

options.text: Getting more useful work from less energy input

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy conservation means:

options.text: Reducing energy use through behavioral changes (turning off lights, carpooling)

quiz_questions.question_text: LED bulbs use approximately _____ less energy than incandescent bulbs.
Cogeneration (CHP) achieves ~_____ total efficiency by using waste heat.
The most cost-effective way to reduce energy demand is typically:

options.text: Improving energy efficiency in existing buildings, appliances, and vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: Cheapest energy is energy not used.

quiz_questions.question_text: A negawatt represents:

options.text: A unit of energy saved through efficiency measures (often cheaper than generating new energy)

quiz_questions.question_text: ENERGY STAR labels indicate:

options.text: Products that meet or exceed federal energy efficiency standards

quiz_questions.question_text: Building insulation reduces energy use by:

options.text: Slowing heat transfer, reducing heating and cooling energy demand

quiz_questions.question_text: Transportation accounts for ~_____ of US energy consumption.
Electric vehicles (EVs) are more efficient than gasoline cars because:

options.text: Electric motors convert ~85-90% of electrical energy to motion vs. ~20-40% for internal combustion engines

quiz_questions.question_text: CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) standards require:

options.text: Automakers to meet minimum fuel efficiency averages across their vehicle fleet

quiz_questions.question_text: Green building standards (LEED) promote:

options.text: Energy efficiency, sustainable materials, water conservation, and indoor air quality in construction

quiz_questions.question_text: Passive solar design uses:

options.text: Building orientation, windows, thermal mass, and insulation to naturally heat and cool buildings without active systems

quiz_questions.question_text: Smart thermostats save energy by:

options.text: Learning usage patterns and automatically adjusting temperature when occupants are away or asleep

quiz_questions.question_text: Industrial energy efficiency can be improved through:

options.text: Process optimization, waste heat recovery, variable speed motors, and better controls

quiz_questions.question_text: Jevons Paradox (rebound effect) suggests that:

options.text: Increased efficiency can lead to increased total consumption because lower cost per unit encourages more use

quiz_questions.question_text: Combined cycle gas turbines achieve higher efficiency by:

options.text: Using waste heat from the gas turbine to generate steam for a second turbine (~60% efficiency vs. ~33%)

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy audits help identify:

options.text: Where a building or facility is wasting energy, prioritizing the most cost-effective efficiency improvements

quiz_questions.question_text: Behavioral change programs (like eco-driving) can reduce fuel use by:

options.text: 5-15% (proper tire pressure, smooth acceleration, reduced idling — easy, free savings)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, understand that energy efficiency is important because:

options.text: It reduces environmental impact, saves money, improves energy security, and is the fastest way to cut emissions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Energy Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Energy Policy

Germany's Energiewende

Ambitious transition to renewables. ~50% of electricity from renewables (2023). Also phased out nuclear. Higher costs; criticized for coal persistence during transition.

Iceland

~100% of electricity from renewables (geothermal + hydro), thanks to abundant volcanic activity. Model for renewable-powered society.

US Energy Policy

Mix of federal/state policies: clean energy tax credits, CAFE standards, state renewable portfolio standards. Inflation Reduction Act (2022) largest US climate investment.

flashcards.definition: Germany's energy transition policy: ambitious shift from fossil fuels and nuclear to renewable energy.
State-level US policy requiring utilities to source a minimum percentage of electricity from renewables.
Policy guaranteeing renewable energy producers a fixed price per kWh, incentivizing investment.
Tax on fossil fuel carbon content designed to internalize the cost of CO₂ emissions.
Largest US climate investment: ~$370 billion in clean energy tax credits and incentives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Germany's Energiewende aims to:

options.text: Transition from fossil fuels and nuclear to renewable energy sources

quiz_questions.question_text: Germany generates approximately _____ of its electricity from renewables (2023).
A criticism of Germany's Energiewende is:

options.text: Increased reliance on coal during the nuclear phase-out, partially offsetting emission reductions

quiz_questions.question_text: Iceland generates ~100% of its electricity from:
Iceland's renewable success is largely due to:

options.text: Abundant geothermal resources from volcanic activity and glacial rivers for hydropower

quiz_questions.question_text: A renewable portfolio standard (RPS) requires:

options.text: Utilities to source a minimum percentage of electricity from renewable sources by a target date

quiz_questions.question_text: Feed-in tariffs encourage renewable investment by:

options.text: Guaranteeing producers a fixed, above-market price per kWh of renewable electricity

quiz_questions.question_text: A carbon tax works by:

options.text: Making fossil fuels more expensive by taxing their carbon content → incentivizing clean alternatives

quiz_questions.question_text: The Inflation Reduction Act (2022) includes approximately:

options.text: ~$370 billion in clean energy tax credits, incentives, and climate investments

quiz_questions.question_text: Denmark aims for _____ of its electricity from wind by 2030.

options.text: ~80%+ (already a global leader in wind energy)

quiz_questions.question_text: China leads the world in:

options.text: Total installed renewable energy capacity (largest solar, wind, and hydropower capacity globally) — though also largest coal user

quiz_questions.question_text: India's solar ambitions include:

options.text: Massive solar park construction (e.g., Bhadla Solar Park) and a target of 500 GW renewable capacity by 2030

quiz_questions.question_text: Costa Rica generates ~98% of electricity from renewables, primarily:

options.text: Hydroelectric, with geothermal and wind supplementing

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas leads the US in wind energy production because of:

options.text: Abundant wind resources, early RPS policy, deregulated energy market, and transmission infrastructure investment

quiz_questions.question_text: California's clean energy policies include:

options.text: 100% clean electricity target by 2045, cap-and-trade, EV mandates, and building efficiency standards

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy subsidies globally favor fossil fuels over renewables by approximately:

options.text: Several times more ($7+ trillion/year in fossil fuel subsidies including health costs vs. much less for renewables)

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of a 'just transition' means:

options.text: Ensuring workers and communities dependent on fossil fuel industries are supported during the energy transition

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy independence through renewables reduces:

options.text: Dependence on imported fossil fuels, geopolitical risk, and price volatility

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, energy policy questions require:

options.text: Comparing policy approaches (taxes, standards, subsidies), evaluating trade-offs, and analyzing real-world outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: The key lesson from these case studies is:

options.text: Successful energy transitions require multiple policies, geography-specific strategies, and long-term commitment

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Energy Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Energy Calculations

Key Formulas

  • Efficiency: η = (useful energy output / total energy input) × 100%
  • Power: P = Energy / Time (watts = joules/seconds)
  • Electrical energy: E = P × t (kWh = kW × hours)
  • Cost: Cost = kWh × price per kWh
  • 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J

flashcards.definition: Useful energy output ÷ total energy input × 100%; always < 100% due to second law of thermodynamics.
Unit of energy: 1 kW of power used for 1 hour; equals 3.6 × 10⁶ joules.
Rate of energy use: 1 watt = 1 joule per second; 1 kilowatt = 1,000 watts.
Actual energy output / maximum possible output × 100% (e.g., solar ~25%, nuclear ~90%, wind ~35%).
Ratio of energy delivered by a source to energy invested to produce it; higher is better.

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy efficiency is calculated as:

options.text: Useful energy output ÷ Total energy input × 100%

quiz_questions.question_text: A power plant converts 1,000 MW of heat into 330 MW of electricity. Efficiency is:
1 kWh equals:
A 100-watt bulb on for 10 hours uses:
If electricity costs $0.12/kWh, running a 2,000W heater for 5 hours costs:
A 5 kW solar panel system with 25% capacity factor produces annually:
The capacity factor of nuclear power is typically ~____%.
The capacity factor of solar PV is typically ~____%.

options.text: ~25% (varies by location; higher in sunny areas like Arizona)

quiz_questions.question_text: If a coal plant has EROI of 30:1, it means:

options.text: For every 1 unit of energy invested, it delivers 30 units (positive net energy)

quiz_questions.question_text: Replacing a 60W incandescent with a 10W LED saves _____ watts.
If you replace 20 incandescent bulbs (60W each) with LEDs (10W each), daily savings (8 hrs) are:
A home uses 30 kWh/day. Annual consumption is:
At $0.15/kWh, annual cost for the 30 kWh/day home is:
Second law efficiency considers:

options.text: How close a process comes to the theoretical maximum efficiency (thermodynamic limit)

quiz_questions.question_text: A heat pump with COP (coefficient of performance) of 3 means it:

options.text: Delivers 3 units of heat for every 1 unit of electricity consumed

quiz_questions.question_text: To convert 500 MW power plant output to kWh per year:

options.text: 500,000 kW × 8,760 hours × capacity factor (e.g., × 0.90 = 3,942,000,000 kWh for nuclear)

quiz_questions.question_text: Per-capita energy consumption is highest in:

options.text: Countries like Iceland, Qatar, and the US (high development + energy-intensive lifestyles)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Sankey diagram shows:

options.text: Energy flow and losses through a system (width of arrows proportional to energy)

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP free-response energy calculation questions, students must:

options.text: Show all work with labeled formulas, correct units, and interpret results in environmental context

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding energy units and calculations is essential because:

options.text: Quantitative reasoning about energy is a core AP skill — calculation questions appear in both MC and FRQ sections

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Air Pollution
summary_sections.content_html:
Air Pollution

Primary Pollutants

Emitted directly: CO, SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter (PM), volatile organic compounds (VOCs), lead.

Secondary Pollutants

Formed by chemical reactions in the atmosphere: ozone (O₃), smog, acid rain.

Key Issues

  • Criteria pollutants: 6 regulated by the Clean Air Act (CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM, SO₂).
  • Indoor air pollution: Radon, VOCs, mold, CO — kills ~3.8 million/year globally (cooking with solid fuels).

flashcards.definition: Pollutant emitted directly into the atmosphere (CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs, lead).
Formed by chemical reactions of primary pollutants in the atmosphere (ozone, smog, acid deposition).
Six pollutants regulated by the Clean Air Act: CO, Pb (lead), NO₂, O₃ (ozone), PM, SO₂.
Brown haze formed when sunlight reacts with NOₓ and VOCs; worst in sunny cities with heavy traffic (LA).
Warm air layer traps cool air and pollutants near the ground, worsening air quality.

quiz_questions.question_text: Primary pollutants are:

options.text: Emitted directly from a source (e.g., CO from cars, SO₂ from power plants)

quiz_questions.question_text: Secondary pollutants are:

options.text: Formed by chemical reactions in the atmosphere (e.g., ground-level ozone from NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight)

quiz_questions.question_text: How many criteria pollutants are regulated by the Clean Air Act?
Photochemical smog forms from:

options.text: NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight → ground-level ozone and other oxidants (brown haze)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ground-level ozone is considered _____ at ground level.

options.text: Harmful (causes respiratory damage — it's the main component of photochemical smog)

quiz_questions.question_text: Thermal inversions worsen air pollution by:

options.text: Trapping pollutants near the ground under a warm air lid (preventing normal convective mixing)

quiz_questions.question_text: Indoor air pollution kills approximately _____ people per year globally.

options.text: ~3.8 million (mainly from cooking with solid fuels in developing countries)

quiz_questions.question_text: Radon is a dangerous indoor pollutant because it:

options.text: Is a colorless, odorless radioactive gas that seeps from soil; 2nd leading cause of lung cancer after smoking

quiz_questions.question_text: SO₂ emissions primarily come from:

options.text: Coal-burning power plants and industrial processes

quiz_questions.question_text: NOₓ emissions come primarily from:

options.text: Vehicle engines and power plants (formed at high combustion temperatures)

quiz_questions.question_text: Acid deposition (acid rain) forms when:

options.text: SO₂ and NOₓ react with water vapor in the atmosphere to form H₂SO₄ and HNO₃

quiz_questions.question_text: Effects of acid rain include:

options.text: Lake acidification, forest damage, soil nutrient leaching, and building/monument corrosion

quiz_questions.question_text: PM2.5 refers to particles:

options.text: Smaller than 2.5 micrometers in diameter (can penetrate deep into lungs and enter bloodstream)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Air Act (1970, US) has led to:

options.text: Significant reductions in criteria pollutants (lead down 99%, SO₂ down ~90%) despite population and GDP growth

quiz_questions.question_text: Lead was removed from gasoline because it:

options.text: Caused neurological damage, especially in children (leaded gas emissions were the primary source of airborne lead)

quiz_questions.question_text: Catalytic converters on cars reduce emissions of:
Industrial scrubbers reduce:

options.text: SO₂ and particulate emissions from smokestacks (removes pollutants before they enter the atmosphere)

quiz_questions.question_text: Air Quality Index (AQI) measures:

options.text: How clean or polluted outdoor air is and associated health effects (0-500 scale; higher = worse)

quiz_questions.question_text: Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) come from:

options.text: Paints, solvents, gasoline, cleaning products, and vehicle emissions (contribute to smog formation)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, air pollution questions involve:

options.text: Identifying pollutant sources and types, explaining formation of secondary pollutants, analyzing health impacts, and evaluating control strategies

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Water Pollution
summary_sections.content_html:
Water Pollution

Types

  • Point source: Single identifiable source (factory pipe, sewage outfall).
  • Non-point source: Diffuse (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater).

Major Pollutants

  • Nutrients: N, P → eutrophication → algal blooms → dead zones.
  • Pathogens: Bacteria, viruses from sewage → cholera, dysentery.
  • Toxics: Heavy metals (Hg, Pb), pesticides, pharmaceuticals.
  • Thermal: Heated discharge from power plants reduces dissolved O₂.

flashcards.definition: Excess nutrient enrichment (N, P) → algal blooms → decomposition depletes oxygen → dead zones.
Amount of O₂ needed by microorganisms to decompose organic matter; high BOD = polluted water.
Area of very low dissolved oxygen (hypoxia) where most aquatic life cannot survive.
Disease-causing organism (bacteria, viruses, parasites) in contaminated water.
Buildup of a persistent chemical in an organism's body over time (e.g., mercury in fish).

quiz_questions.question_text: Point source water pollution comes from:

options.text: A single, identifiable source (e.g., factory discharge pipe, sewage outfall)

quiz_questions.question_text: Non-point source pollution is the _____ type to control.

options.text: Hardest (diffuse sources: agricultural runoff, urban stormwater, atmospheric deposition)

quiz_questions.question_text: Eutrophication is caused by excess:
The sequence of eutrophication is:

options.text: Excess nutrients → algal bloom → algae die → decomposition consumes oxygen → dead zone

quiz_questions.question_text: The Gulf of Mexico dead zone is primarily caused by:

options.text: Agricultural fertilizer runoff from the Mississippi River watershed

quiz_questions.question_text: BOD (Biological Oxygen Demand) indicates:

options.text: How much organic pollution is in water (high BOD = lots of organic waste = less dissolved oxygen)

quiz_questions.question_text: Major waterborne pathogens include:

options.text: Bacteria (cholera, E. coli), viruses (hepatitis A), and parasites (Giardia, Cryptosporidium)

quiz_questions.question_text: Globally, approximately _____ people lack access to safely managed sanitation.
Heavy metals in water (mercury, lead) are dangerous because they:

options.text: Bioaccumulate in organisms and biomagnify up food chains; cause neurological and developmental damage

quiz_questions.question_text: Mercury contamination in fish is primarily from:

options.text: Coal-burning power plants (atmospheric deposition) and gold mining

quiz_questions.question_text: Thermal pollution reduces dissolved oxygen because:

options.text: Warm water holds less dissolved gas — heated discharge from power plants raises water temperature
Fish use more O₂ in cold water

quiz_questions.question_text: Microplastics in water are concerning because they:

options.text: Are tiny (<5mm), persistent, absorb toxins, and are ingested by organisms throughout the food web

quiz_questions.question_text: Pharmaceuticals (antibiotics, hormones) in waterways can:

options.text: Disrupt endocrine systems in aquatic life, contribute to antibiotic resistance; not fully removed by most treatment plants

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Water Act (1972, US) primarily regulates:

options.text: Point source discharges into surface waters (sets discharge limits and water quality standards)

quiz_questions.question_text: Wastewater treatment includes primary (settling), secondary (biological), and _____ treatment.

options.text: Tertiary (advanced filtration, chemical treatment to remove nutrients, pharmaceuticals)

quiz_questions.question_text: Wetlands help purify water by:

options.text: Filtering sediment, absorbing nutrients and toxins, and allowing biological breakdown of pollutants (natural treatment systems)

quiz_questions.question_text: Impervious surfaces in cities increase water pollution by:

options.text: Increasing stormwater runoff that carries oil, metals, trash, and chemicals directly into waterways

quiz_questions.question_text: Green infrastructure solutions for water pollution include:

options.text: Rain gardens, permeable pavement, green roofs, constructed wetlands, and bioswales

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean acidification occurs when CO₂ dissolves in seawater, forming:

options.text: Carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), which lowers pH and threatens shell-forming organisms

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, water pollution questions require:

options.text: Identifying pollution sources (point vs. non-point), describing impacts on ecosystems, and evaluating remediation strategies

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Soil Pollution & Land Degradation
summary_sections.content_html:
Soil Pollution & Land Degradation

Soil contamination comes from industrial waste, pesticides, mining, landfills, and atmospheric deposition. Land degradation affects ~25% of Earth's ice-free land.

Key Issues

  • Brownfields: Former industrial sites with contamination requiring cleanup.
  • Superfund (CERCLA): US law funding cleanup of the worst hazardous waste sites.
  • Bioremediation: Using organisms to break down contaminants (bacteria, fungi, plants).

flashcards.definition: Abandoned or underused industrial/commercial site with real or suspected contamination.
US law (1980) establishing a program to clean up the nation's worst hazardous waste sites.
Using living organisms (bacteria, fungi, plants) to break down or absorb soil and water contaminants.
Using plants to absorb, accumulate, or break down contaminants in soil or water.
Decline in land productivity due to erosion, contamination, salinization, or desertification.

quiz_questions.question_text: Land degradation affects approximately _____ of Earth's ice-free land surface.

options.text: ~25% (and worsening due to agriculture, mining, urbanization)

quiz_questions.question_text: Brownfields are:

options.text: Abandoned or underused industrial sites with real or suspected contamination

quiz_questions.question_text: Superfund (CERCLA, 1980) was created to:

options.text: Fund cleanup of the nation's worst uncontrolled hazardous waste sites

quiz_questions.question_text: The principle of 'polluter pays' means:

options.text: The party responsible for contamination should bear the cleanup costs

quiz_questions.question_text: Bioremediation uses _____ to clean up contaminated soil.

options.text: Living organisms (bacteria, fungi, or plants that break down or absorb pollutants)

quiz_questions.question_text: Phytoremediation specifically uses:

options.text: Plants (e.g., sunflowers to absorb heavy metals, willows for water filtration)

quiz_questions.question_text: Advantages of bioremediation over traditional cleanup include:

options.text: Lower cost, less disruptive, in-situ treatment (no soil removal), and more environmentally friendly

quiz_questions.question_text: Pesticide contamination in soil can:

options.text: Persist for years, leach into groundwater, harm soil organisms, and enter the food chain

quiz_questions.question_text: Heavy metals in soil (lead, cadmium, arsenic) are particularly dangerous because they:

options.text: Are persistent (don't break down), bioaccumulate, and are toxic to organisms and humans

quiz_questions.question_text: Mining waste (tailings) can contaminate soil through:
