options.text: Acid mine drainage (sulfuric acid from exposed minerals), heavy metal leaching, and windblown dust

quiz_questions.question_text: Landfills can contaminate surrounding soil and groundwater through:

options.text: Leachate (contaminated liquid that seeps through waste and can escape the landfill liner)

quiz_questions.question_text: Modern sanitary landfills differ from open dumps by having:

options.text: Liners (clay + plastic), leachate collection systems, methane capture, and daily cover

quiz_questions.question_text: Soil salinization from irrigation can be reduced by:

options.text: Drip irrigation, better drainage, growing salt-tolerant crops, and allowing fields to rest

quiz_questions.question_text: Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) include:

options.text: DDT, PCBs, dioxins — chemicals that persist in the environment, bioaccumulate, and are toxic

quiz_questions.question_text: The Stockholm Convention (2001) aims to eliminate or restrict:

options.text: Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) because of their global transport and bioaccumulation

quiz_questions.question_text: Love Canal (1978) was a landmark case because:

options.text: A neighborhood built on a buried chemical waste dump had widespread illness → led to Superfund legislation

quiz_questions.question_text: Soil contamination disproportionately affects:

options.text: Low-income and minority communities (environmental justice issue — toxic sites often located in disadvantaged areas)

quiz_questions.question_text: Composting reduces landfill waste by:

options.text: Diverting organic matter from landfills and converting it to nutrient-rich soil amendment

quiz_questions.question_text: Contaminated soil can be treated by thermal desorption, which:

options.text: Heats soil to vaporize contaminants for collection and treatment

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, soil pollution connects to:

options.text: Agriculture, water quality, human health, environmental justice, waste management, and policy

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solid Waste Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Solid Waste Management

The US generates ~292 million tons of municipal solid waste (MSW) annually (~4.9 lbs/person/day). Globally: ~2 billion tons/year.

Hierarchy (most to least preferred)

  • Reduce → Reuse → Recycle/Compost → Energy recovery → Landfill

US recycles ~32% of MSW. Landfills remain the primary disposal method despite methane emissions.

flashcards.definition: Everyday trash generated by households and businesses; ~292 million tons/year in the US.
Preferred order: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle/Compost → Energy Recovery → Landfill (least preferred).
Percentage of waste diverted from landfills through recycling and composting; US ~32%.
Incineration of waste to generate electricity; reduces landfill volume but produces ash and emissions.
Discarded electronics containing valuable metals and hazardous materials; fastest-growing waste stream.

quiz_questions.question_text: The US generates approximately _____ of MSW per person per day.
The waste management hierarchy prioritizes (most to least preferred):

options.text: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle/Compost → Energy Recovery → Landfill

quiz_questions.question_text: The US recycling rate is approximately:
Landfills are the _____ preferred option in the waste hierarchy.

options.text: Least (last resort after reduce, reuse, recycle, and energy recovery)

quiz_questions.question_text: Modern sanitary landfills have liners to prevent:

options.text: Leachate (contaminated liquid) from contaminating groundwater and surrounding soil

quiz_questions.question_text: Landfill methane is generated by:

options.text: Anaerobic (oxygen-free) decomposition of organic waste by bacteria

quiz_questions.question_text: Landfill methane can be captured and used for:

options.text: Energy generation (methane is essentially natural gas and can generate electricity or heat)

quiz_questions.question_text: Waste-to-energy (WTE) incineration reduces waste volume by:
Concerns about WTE incineration include:

options.text: Air emissions (dioxins, heavy metals, particulates), toxic ash disposal, and discouraging recycling

quiz_questions.question_text: E-waste (electronic waste) is the fastest-growing waste stream because:

options.text: Rapid technology turnover and short product lifespans create massive volumes of discarded electronics

quiz_questions.question_text: E-waste contains both valuable materials (gold, copper) and:

options.text: Hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, flame retardants) requiring careful handling

quiz_questions.question_text: Much of the world's e-waste is exported to:

options.text: Developing countries (e.g., Ghana, China, India) where informal recycling exposes workers to toxic materials

quiz_questions.question_text: The circular economy model aims to:

options.text: Design products for durability, repairability, reuse, and recycling — eliminating waste entirely

quiz_questions.question_text: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) requires:

options.text: Manufacturers to bear responsibility for end-of-life management of their products

quiz_questions.question_text: Single-use plastics are problematic because they:

options.text: Are used briefly but persist in the environment for hundreds of years, polluting oceans and land

quiz_questions.question_text: Pay-as-you-throw (PAYT) programs charge residents:

options.text: Based on the amount of waste they generate — incentivizing waste reduction and recycling

quiz_questions.question_text: Composting diverts approximately _____ of MSW that is organic material.

options.text: ~30% (food scraps and yard waste are large portions of MSW)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean plastic pollution is estimated at _____ tons entering oceans annually.
Zero-waste communities aim to:

options.text: Divert >90% of waste from landfills through aggressive reduction, reuse, recycling, and composting

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, waste management questions connect to:

options.text: Resource use, pollution (air, water, soil), environmental justice, policy, and sustainability

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hazardous Waste & Recycling
summary_sections.content_html:
Hazardous Waste & Recycling

Hazardous waste: Waste that is ignitable, corrosive, reactive, or toxic. Regulated by RCRA (Resource Conservation and Recovery Act, 1976).

Recycling

Reduces need for virgin resources, saves energy (recycling aluminum uses 95% less energy than new), and reduces landfill burden. Challenges: contamination, market economics, single-stream confusion.

flashcards.definition: Waste with properties that make it dangerous: ignitable, corrosive, reactive, or toxic.
Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (1976); regulates hazardous waste from cradle to grave in the US.
RCRA system tracking hazardous waste from generation through transport, treatment, storage, and disposal.
All recyclables placed in one bin; convenient but increases contamination (non-recyclable items mixed in).
Recycling material into a lower-quality product (e.g., plastic bottles → carpet fiber); common for plastics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hazardous waste is defined as waste that is:

options.text: Ignitable, corrosive, reactive, or toxic (RCRA definition)

quiz_questions.question_text: RCRA (1976) regulates hazardous waste:

options.text: From cradle to grave (generation → transport → treatment → storage → disposal)

quiz_questions.question_text: Recycling aluminum saves approximately _____ of the energy needed to make new aluminum.
Recycling paper saves approximately _____ of the energy compared to making new paper.
Single-stream recycling increases convenience but also increases:

options.text: Contamination (when non-recyclable items or food waste are mixed in, entire batches may be landfilled)

quiz_questions.question_text: Downcycling means:

options.text: Recycling into a lower-quality product (e.g., mixed plastics → park benches; less valuable than original)

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest challenge for plastics recycling is:

options.text: Most plastics can only be downcycled, and many types are not economically recyclable (only ~5-6% of US plastics are recycled)

quiz_questions.question_text: Wish-cycling (putting non-recyclable items in recycling bins) leads to:

options.text: Contamination that can cause entire loads to be sent to landfill instead of being recycled

quiz_questions.question_text: Chemical recycling of plastics aims to:

options.text: Break plastics back into monomers or feedstock chemicals, enabling true recycling into same-quality products

quiz_questions.question_text: PCBs (polychlorinated biphenyls) are hazardous because they:

options.text: Are persistent, bioaccumulate, and cause cancer and other health problems (banned in US in 1979 but still present)

quiz_questions.question_text: Proper hazardous waste disposal includes:

options.text: Secure chemical landfills with multiple liners, leak detection, and monitoring — or treatment (incineration, neutralization)

quiz_questions.question_text: Deep-well injection involves:

options.text: Pumping liquid hazardous waste deep underground into permeable rock formations sealed from aquifers

quiz_questions.question_text: The Basel Convention (1989) restricts:

options.text: International movement of hazardous waste (prevents rich nations from dumping in poor nations)

quiz_questions.question_text: Radioactive waste requires isolation for:

options.text: Thousands to hundreds of thousands of years (high-level waste)

quiz_questions.question_text: Glass recycling is efficient because glass can be recycled:

options.text: Infinitely (glass doesn't lose quality through recycling)

quiz_questions.question_text: Metal recycling is economically viable because:

options.text: Mining and refining virgin ores is energy-intensive and environmentally destructive; recycled metals save significant energy

quiz_questions.question_text: Composting organic waste is preferable to landfilling because:

options.text: Composting creates useful soil amendment and avoids methane emissions from anaerobic landfill decomposition

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'cradle to cradle' design means:

options.text: Products are designed so all materials can be fully recovered and reused in new products (eliminating waste)

quiz_questions.question_text: Illegal dumping of hazardous waste is a problem because:

options.text: It saves money for violators but causes severe environmental contamination and public health risks in affected communities

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, hazardous waste and recycling questions often involve:

options.text: Comparing disposal methods, analyzing policy effectiveness, evaluating environmental justice issues, and calculating energy savings

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Pollution Control
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Pollution Control

US Acid Rain Program (1990 Clean Air Act Amendments)

Cap-and-trade for SO₂ emissions. Reduced SO₂ by ~90% since 1990. Cost far less than predicted. Model for pollution regulation.

London Smog (1952)

Great Smog killed ~12,000 people. Led to UK's Clean Air Act (1956). Spurred global air quality awareness.

Flint Water Crisis (2014)

Water source switch corroded lead pipes → lead contamination. Disproportionately affected low-income, majority-Black community. Environmental justice failure.

flashcards.definition: US cap-and-trade program for SO₂ under 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments; reduced SO₂ by ~90%.
1952 killer smog (coal smoke + fog) that killed ~12,000 people; led to UK Clean Air Act.
2014 lead contamination when Flint, MI switched water sources; a major environmental justice failure.
Market-based system: government caps total pollutant emissions; companies trade emission allowances.
The fair treatment of all people regardless of race, income, or origin in environmental policy and protection.

quiz_questions.question_text: The US Acid Rain Program reduced SO₂ emissions by approximately:
The Acid Rain Program used a _____ approach.

options.text: Cap-and-trade (set a cap on total SO₂ emissions; companies trade emission allowances)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Acid Rain Program cost _____ than initially predicted.

options.text: Much less (industry found cheaper ways to reduce emissions than regulators anticipated)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Great Smog of London (1952) killed approximately:

options.text: ~12,000 people (from respiratory and cardiovascular causes over several months)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Great Smog was caused by:

options.text: Coal smoke + cold weather + windless fog trapping pollutants (high SO₂ and PM concentrations)

quiz_questions.question_text: The UK Clean Air Act (1956) was passed directly because of:

options.text: The Great Smog catastrophe, which made air pollution regulation politically urgent

quiz_questions.question_text: The Flint Water Crisis occurred when:

options.text: The city switched water sources to save money; the new (more corrosive) water leached lead from aging pipes

quiz_questions.question_text: The Flint crisis disproportionately affected:

options.text: A low-income, majority-Black community — highlighting systemic environmental injustice

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental justice means:

options.text: Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, income, or origin in environmental decisions

quiz_questions.question_text: The Cuyahoga River (Cleveland) caught fire in 1969 because:

options.text: It was so polluted with industrial chemicals and oil that it was literally flammable

quiz_questions.question_text: The Cuyahoga River fire contributed to passage of:

options.text: The Clean Water Act (1972) and creation of the EPA (1970)

quiz_questions.question_text: Bhopal disaster (1984, India) involved:

options.text: A toxic gas leak (methyl isocyanate) from a Union Carbide pesticide plant, killing thousands

quiz_questions.question_text: Bhopal's death toll is estimated at:

options.text: 3,800–16,000+ (with ongoing health effects for hundreds of thousands)

quiz_questions.question_text: Love Canal (1978) demonstrated:

options.text: That burying hazardous waste does not make it disappear — chemicals migrated into homes and schools

quiz_questions.question_text: Minamata disease (Japan, 1950s) was caused by:

options.text: Industrial mercury discharge into Minamata Bay → bioaccumulation in fish → severe neurological damage in people

quiz_questions.question_text: These case studies share the pattern of:

options.text: Environmental harm being discovered only after significant damage — highlighting the need for prevention and precaution

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental regulation typically follows:

options.text: Reactive response to crises (disasters → public outrage → legislation) — though this is changing

quiz_questions.question_text: The lesson from the Acid Rain Program is that:

options.text: Well-designed market-based policies can achieve environmental goals more cheaply than command-and-control regulation

quiz_questions.question_text: The lesson from Flint is that:

options.text: Aging infrastructure, environmental racism, and regulatory failure can create public health disasters — equity must be part of environmental policy

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, pollution case studies require:

options.text: Analyzing causes, identifying stakeholders, evaluating policy responses, and connecting to broader environmental principles

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Pollution Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Pollution Models

Key Concepts for Calculations

  • LD50: Dose lethal to 50% of test organisms; measures acute toxicity.
  • Dose-response curve: Plots health effect vs. exposure dose; determines threshold and no-observed-adverse-effect level (NOAEL).
  • Bioaccumulation factor: Concentration in organism / concentration in environment.
  • Biomagnification factor: Concentration at higher trophic level / concentration at lower level.
  • Half-life: Time for pollutant concentration to decrease by half.

flashcards.definition: Lethal Dose 50: the dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population; measure of acute toxicity.
Graph showing the relationship between exposure dose and biological response/health effect.
No Observed Adverse Effect Level: highest dose with no measurable harmful effect.
Ratio of pollutant concentration in an organism to its concentration in the surrounding environment.
Time for half of a pollutant to break down or be removed from the environment.

quiz_questions.question_text: LD50 measures:

options.text: The lethal dose for all organisms
The dose that kills 50% of a test population (lower LD50 = more toxic)

quiz_questions.question_text: A substance with a lower LD50 is:

options.text: More toxic (it takes less of the substance to kill 50% of organisms)

quiz_questions.question_text: If substance A has LD50 = 5 mg/kg and substance B has LD50 = 500 mg/kg, then:

options.text: A is more toxic (100× more toxic; requires much less to kill 50%)

quiz_questions.question_text: A dose-response curve shows:

options.text: The relationship between increasing exposure (dose) and the severity of biological response

quiz_questions.question_text: The threshold in a dose-response curve is:

options.text: The dose below which no measurable effect is observed (though some scientists argue no safe threshold exists for carcinogens)

quiz_questions.question_text: NOAEL stands for:

options.text: No Observed Adverse Effect Level (highest dose with no measurable harmful effect)
None Of the Above Effects Listed

quiz_questions.question_text: Bioaccumulation factor is calculated as:

options.text: Concentration in organism ÷ concentration in environment

quiz_questions.question_text: If mercury in water is 0.001 ppm and in a fish is 1 ppm, the bioaccumulation factor is:
Biomagnification factor is:

options.text: Concentration at higher trophic level ÷ concentration at lower trophic level

quiz_questions.question_text: If DDT in zooplankton is 0.04 ppm and in fish is 0.5 ppm, the biomagnification factor is:
The half-life of a pollutant is:

options.text: Time for half of the pollutant to break down or be removed

quiz_questions.question_text: If a pesticide has a half-life of 30 days and initial concentration is 100 ppm, after 90 days:
Persistent pollutants (long half-lives) are more dangerous because they:

options.text: Remain in the environment longer, increasing exposure time and bioaccumulation potential

quiz_questions.question_text: Synergistic effects occur when two pollutants together cause:

options.text: Worse effects than the sum of their individual effects (1+1 > 2)

quiz_questions.question_text: Antagonistic effects occur when one substance _____ the effects of another.

options.text: Reduces (e.g., a treatment that counteracts a poison)

quiz_questions.question_text: Acute exposure refers to:

options.text: Short-term, high-dose exposure to a substance

quiz_questions.question_text: Chronic exposure refers to:

options.text: Long-term, low-dose exposure over weeks to years

quiz_questions.question_text: Risk assessment involves:

options.text: Hazard identification, dose-response assessment, exposure assessment, and risk characterization (4 steps)

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP calculations, students should show:

options.text: All work: formula used, correct substitution, correct units, and environmental interpretation

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding toxicology and pollution models is essential for AP because:

options.text: Questions on LD50, dose-response, bioaccumulation, and biomagnification appear regularly in both MC and FRQ

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Greenhouse Effect & Global Warming
summary_sections.content_html:
Greenhouse Effect & Global Warming

The natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth ~33°C warmer than it would be without an atmosphere (−18°C → +15°C). Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, CFCs) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation.

Enhanced Greenhouse Effect

Human activities (fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture) have increased CO₂ from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to ~424 ppm (2024). Global temperature has risen ~1.1°C above pre-industrial levels.

flashcards.definition: Natural process: greenhouse gases trap infrared radiation, warming Earth ~33°C above what it would be without an atmosphere.
Human-caused increase in greenhouse gas concentrations intensifying the natural greenhouse effect → global warming.
Pre-industrial: ~280 ppm; 2024: ~424 ppm — a 50%+ increase primarily from fossil fuel burning and deforestation.
Change in net energy balance at the top of the atmosphere; positive forcing warms, negative cools.
Measure comparing a greenhouse gas's heat-trapping ability to CO₂ over a set period (e.g., CH₄ GWP ≈ 80 over 20 years).

quiz_questions.question_text: The natural greenhouse effect makes Earth approximately _____ warmer than it would be otherwise.
The primary greenhouse gases are:

options.text: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, water vapor, and CFCs

quiz_questions.question_text: Pre-industrial CO₂ concentration was approximately:
Current CO₂ concentration (2024) is approximately:
The largest source of anthropogenic CO₂ is:

options.text: Fossil fuel combustion (coal, oil, natural gas for energy and transport)

quiz_questions.question_text: Methane (CH₄) is a more potent greenhouse gas than CO₂ because it:

options.text: Traps ~80× more heat per molecule over 20 years (though it's shorter-lived in the atmosphere)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sources of anthropogenic methane include:

options.text: Livestock (enteric fermentation), rice paddies, landfills, fossil fuel extraction, and wetland changes

quiz_questions.question_text: N₂O (nitrous oxide) comes primarily from:

options.text: Agricultural fertilizers and soil management (nitrogen cycle disruption)

quiz_questions.question_text: CFCs are greenhouse gases AND they also:
Water vapor is the most abundant greenhouse gas but is not directly controlled because:

options.text: Its concentration is determined by temperature (it amplifies warming as a feedback, not a driver)

quiz_questions.question_text: Global average temperature has risen approximately _____ above pre-industrial levels.
The Keeling Curve shows:

options.text: The continuous rise of atmospheric CO₂ since 1958 (measured at Mauna Loa, Hawaii)

quiz_questions.question_text: Positive feedback loops in climate include:

options.text: Ice-albedo feedback (melting ice → less reflection → more warming → more melting) and water vapor amplification

quiz_questions.question_text: The albedo effect refers to:

options.text: Reflectivity of a surface (ice/snow have high albedo; dark ocean has low albedo)

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon sinks include:

options.text: Oceans, forests, and soils (they absorb more CO₂ than they release)

quiz_questions.question_text: Radiative forcing is positive when it _____ Earth's energy balance.

options.text: Increases (more energy retained than lost → warming)

quiz_questions.question_text: The greenhouse effect is essential for life because without it:

options.text: Earth's average temperature would be ~−18°C (too cold for liquid water and most life)

quiz_questions.question_text: CO₂ remains in the atmosphere for approximately:

options.text: Centuries to millennia (a significant portion persists for 300-1,000+ years)

quiz_questions.question_text: The relationship between CO₂ and temperature over geological time is:

options.text: Strongly correlated — ice core records show CO₂ and temperature have risen and fallen together for 800,000+ years

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, students must understand that the greenhouse effect is:

options.text: A natural, essential process that has been enhanced by human activities to dangerous levels

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Evidence of Climate Change
summary_sections.content_html:
Evidence of Climate Change

Multiple Lines of Evidence

  • Temperature records: Instrumental records since 1850; 10 warmest years all since 2010.
  • Ice cores: Trapped air bubbles show CO₂/temperature correlation over 800,000 years.
  • Sea level rise: ~20 cm since 1900; accelerating (~3.6 mm/year now).
  • Arctic ice decline: Summer Arctic sea ice volume down ~75% since 1979.
  • Ocean warming & acidification: Oceans absorbed ~90% of excess heat and ~30% of CO₂.

flashcards.definition: Cylinders of ice drilled from glaciers that contain trapped air bubbles recording past atmospheric composition.
Global sea levels have risen ~20 cm since 1900; caused by thermal expansion and ice sheet melting.
Water expands as it warms; accounts for ~50% of observed sea level rise.
Oceans absorb CO₂, forming carbonic acid; pH has dropped ~0.1 units (30% more acidic) since pre-industrial times.
Indirect evidence of past climate from tree rings, ice cores, coral, and sediment (before instrument records).

quiz_questions.question_text: The 10 warmest years on record have all occurred since:
Ice cores provide climate data going back approximately:
Ice cores contain _____ that reveal past atmospheric composition.
Global sea level has risen approximately _____ since 1900.
Current rate of sea level rise is approximately:
Sea level rise is caused primarily by:

options.text: Thermal expansion of warming ocean water AND melting of land-based ice (glaciers, ice sheets)

quiz_questions.question_text: Summer Arctic sea ice volume has declined approximately _____ since 1979.
Oceans have absorbed approximately _____ of the excess heat from global warming.

options.text: ~90% (oceans are a massive heat sink, masking atmospheric warming)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean acidification occurs because:

options.text: The ocean absorbs CO₂, which reacts with water to form carbonic acid, lowering pH

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean pH has decreased by approximately _____ since pre-industrial times.

options.text: ~0.1 units (which represents a ~30% increase in acidity — pH is logarithmic)

quiz_questions.question_text: Coral bleaching is intensifying due to:

options.text: Warming ocean temperatures causing corals to expel symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae)

quiz_questions.question_text: Glacier retreat worldwide is evidence of warming because:

options.text: Nearly all glaciers globally are losing mass, consistent with rising temperatures

quiz_questions.question_text: Permafrost thawing in the Arctic is concerning because it:

options.text: Releases stored methane and CO₂ (a positive feedback that accelerates warming)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tree ring data (dendrochronology) provides evidence of:

options.text: Past temperature and precipitation patterns (wider rings = favorable growth; narrower = stress)

quiz_questions.question_text: The scientific consensus on climate change is supported by:

options.text: ~97%+ of climate scientists and every major scientific organization worldwide

quiz_questions.question_text: The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) concluded that:

options.text: It is unequivocal that human activities have warmed the atmosphere, ocean, and land

quiz_questions.question_text: Phenological changes (earlier spring events) are evidence because:

options.text: Plants flowering, birds migrating, and insects emerging earlier indicate warming temperatures

quiz_questions.question_text: Satellite data since 1979 confirms:

options.text: Arctic ice decline, sea level rise, shifting precipitation patterns, and global temperature increases

quiz_questions.question_text: Attribution science can now determine:

options.text: The human contribution to specific extreme weather events (e.g., heatwave likelihood multiplied by climate change)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, students should cite _____ lines of evidence for climate change.

options.text: Multiple independent lines (temperature records, ice cores, sea level, ice decline, ocean changes, biological changes)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Impacts on Ecosystems & Humans
summary_sections.content_html:
Climate Change Impacts

Ecosystems

  • Range shifts: species moving poleward and to higher elevations.
  • Coral bleaching: 14% of coral lost 2009–2018.
  • Phenology disruption: mismatches between species (pollinators arriving at wrong time).

Humans

  • Extreme weather: more intense heat waves, droughts, hurricanes, and floods.
  • Food security: crop yield declines; fisheries shifting.
  • Health: heat-related illness, vector-borne diseases expanding range, air quality worsening.
  • Climate refugees: displacement from sea level rise, drought, and extreme weather.

flashcards.definition: Species moving to higher latitudes or elevations in response to warming temperatures.
When timing of ecological events (flowering, migration) becomes out of sync between interdependent species.
Person displaced by climate-related environmental changes (sea level rise, drought, extreme weather).
Reliable access to sufficient, safe, nutritious food; threatened by changing precipitation, temperature, and extreme events.
Diseases transmitted by organisms like mosquitoes and ticks; expanding range as warm areas expand (malaria, dengue, Lyme).

quiz_questions.question_text: Species responding to warming are generally moving:

options.text: Poleward and to higher elevations (tracking their climate envelope)

quiz_questions.question_text: Phenological mismatch means:

options.text: The timing of ecological events (e.g., flowering vs. pollinator arrival) becomes out of sync due to differential responses to warming

quiz_questions.question_text: Coral bleaching has led to loss of approximately _____ of the world's coral since 2009.
Climate change is expected to _____ the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events.

options.text: Increase (more intense heat waves, droughts, hurricanes, heavy rainfall, and flooding)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sea level rise threatens approximately _____ of the world's population living in low-lying coastal areas.
Climate change affects food security through:

options.text: Changing precipitation, increased heat stress on crops, more extreme weather, shifting pest ranges, and water scarcity

quiz_questions.question_text: Vector-borne diseases are expanding because:

options.text: Warmer temperatures expand the range of mosquitoes and ticks (malaria, dengue, Lyme disease moving to new areas)

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate refugees are people displaced by:

options.text: Climate-related events: sea level rise, desertification, extreme weather, and water scarcity

quiz_questions.question_text: Small island nations face existential threats from:

options.text: Sea level rise that could submerge entire countries (e.g., Tuvalu, Marshall Islands, Maldives)

quiz_questions.question_text: Arctic ecosystems are warming _____ the global average.
Polar bears are threatened because:

options.text: Declining Arctic sea ice reduces their hunting platform for seals (their primary food)

quiz_questions.question_text: Ocean warming reduces fishery productivity because:

options.text: Warmer water holds less oxygen and disrupts nutrient upwelling → reduced fish populations and range shifts

quiz_questions.question_text: Heat-related mortality is increasing, especially among:

options.text: Elderly, outdoor workers, urban poor, and people with pre-existing health conditions

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate change worsens air quality because:

options.text: Higher temperatures increase ground-level ozone formation, and wildfires (more frequent) produce PM2.5

quiz_questions.question_text: Mountain glaciers melting threatens water supply for:

options.text: Billions of people who depend on glacial meltwater for drinking, irrigation, and hydropower (Himalayas, Andes, Alps)

quiz_questions.question_text: The economic cost of climate change includes:

options.text: Damage from extreme weather, agriculture losses, health costs, infrastructure damage, and productivity loss (trillions of dollars projected)

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate change disproportionately affects:

options.text: Developing nations and vulnerable communities who contributed least to emissions (environmental injustice)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tipping points in climate systems are:

options.text: Thresholds beyond which change becomes irreversible and self-reinforcing (e.g., ice sheet collapse, Amazon dieback)

quiz_questions.question_text: Biodiversity loss from climate change is compounded by:

options.text: Habitat destruction, pollution, and overexploitation — climate change adds pressure to already stressed ecosystems

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, impact questions require connecting:

options.text: Climate change to specific ecological, social, economic, and health outcomes with cause-and-effect reasoning

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mitigation & Adaptation
summary_sections.content_html:
Mitigation & Adaptation

Mitigation (reducing causes)

  • Transition to renewable energy.
  • Energy efficiency improvements.
  • Carbon pricing (tax or cap-and-trade).
  • Reforestation and soil carbon sequestration.

Adaptation (adjusting to impacts)

  • Sea walls and flood barriers.
  • Drought-resistant crops.
  • Urban cooling (green infrastructure, reflective surfaces).
  • Early warning systems for extreme weather.

flashcards.definition: Actions that reduce the sources or enhance the sinks of greenhouse gases (addressing the cause).
Actions that adjust to current or expected climate change impacts (addressing the effects).
Capturing and storing CO₂ from the atmosphere (forests, soils, carbon capture technology).
Making fossil fuels more expensive through taxes or cap-and-trade to reflect their climate costs.
The ability of a system (ecosystem, community) to withstand and recover from climate impacts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mitigation addresses climate change by:

options.text: Reducing greenhouse gas emissions or increasing carbon sinks (tackling the root cause)

quiz_questions.question_text: Adaptation addresses climate change by:

options.text: Adjusting human and natural systems to cope with current and expected climate impacts

quiz_questions.question_text: Both mitigation and adaptation are needed because:

options.text: Some warming is already locked in (requiring adaptation), but limiting future warming requires emission reductions (mitigation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Examples of mitigation include:

options.text: Transitioning to renewable energy, improving efficiency, carbon pricing, and reforestation

quiz_questions.question_text: Examples of adaptation include:

options.text: Sea walls, drought-resistant crops, urban cooling, wildfire management, and early warning systems

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon sequestration includes:

options.text: Reforestation, soil carbon management, ocean fertilization, and engineered carbon capture and storage (CCS)

quiz_questions.question_text: A carbon tax works by:

options.text: Putting a price on CO₂ emissions, making fossil fuels more expensive and clean alternatives more competitive

quiz_questions.question_text: Cap-and-trade differs from a carbon tax by:

options.text: Setting a cap on total emissions and allowing companies to trade emission allowances (the market sets the price)

quiz_questions.question_text: Nature-based solutions for climate include:

options.text: Protecting and restoring forests, wetlands, mangroves, and grasslands (which naturally absorb CO₂)

quiz_questions.question_text: Green infrastructure for urban adaptation includes:

options.text: Green roofs, urban forests, permeable pavements, and reflective surfaces to reduce heat island effect and flooding

quiz_questions.question_text: Drought-resistant crop development is a form of:

options.text: Adaptation (adjusting agricultural systems to cope with changing precipitation patterns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Early warning systems for extreme weather are:

options.text: Adaptation (protecting lives through advance preparation and evacuation for anticipated climate events)

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) captures CO₂ from:

options.text: Power plant emissions or directly from the atmosphere, then stores it underground

quiz_questions.question_text: Direct Air Capture (DAC) is a technology that:

options.text: Removes CO₂ directly from ambient air using chemical processes (currently expensive but scaling)

quiz_questions.question_text: Managed retreat involves:

options.text: Relocating communities and infrastructure away from areas threatened by sea level rise or other climate hazards

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate-smart agriculture includes:

options.text: Practices that increase productivity, enhance resilience, and reduce emissions (cover crops, no-till, efficient irrigation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Geoengineering proposals include:

options.text: Solar radiation management (reflecting sunlight) and carbon dioxide removal — controversial due to risks and unintended consequences

quiz_questions.question_text: The Paris Agreement goal implies limiting warming to:

options.text: Well below 2°C, preferably 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels

quiz_questions.question_text: Individual actions for mitigation include:

options.text: Reducing energy use, eating less meat, driving less, flying less, and voting for climate policy

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, students should distinguish between and give examples of:

options.text: Both mitigation (reducing causes) and adaptation (adjusting to effects) strategies

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: International Agreements (Kyoto, Paris)
summary_sections.content_html:
International Climate Agreements

Kyoto Protocol (1997)

First binding agreement to reduce GHG emissions. Required developed nations to reduce emissions ~5% below 1990 levels. US never ratified. Mixed results.

Paris Agreement (2015)

Nearly every nation (196 parties). Goal: limit warming to well below 2°C, preferably 1.5°C. Countries set own targets (NDCs — Nationally Determined Contributions). Not legally binding. Updated every 5 years.

flashcards.definition: 1997 international treaty requiring developed nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions ~5% below 1990 levels.
2015 global climate accord: nearly all nations committed to limiting warming to well below 2°C, preferably 1.5°C.
Nationally Determined Contributions: each country's self-set climate targets under the Paris Agreement.
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change: the parent treaty for Kyoto and Paris agreements.
Principle that all nations share climate responsibility but developed nations bear greater obligation due to historical emissions.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Kyoto Protocol (1997) was the first binding treaty to:

options.text: Reduce greenhouse gas emissions (required developed nations to cut emissions ~5% below 1990 levels)

quiz_questions.question_text: The US _____ the Kyoto Protocol.

options.text: Never ratified (signed but never approved by the Senate)

quiz_questions.question_text: A key limitation of Kyoto was that it:

options.text: Only required emission reductions from developed nations; excluded developing nations like China and India

quiz_questions.question_text: The Paris Agreement (2015) was signed by:

options.text: Nearly every nation in the world (196 parties)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Paris Agreement's temperature goal is:

options.text: Well below 2°C, preferably 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels

quiz_questions.question_text: NDCs (Nationally Determined Contributions) are:

options.text: Each country's self-set climate targets under Paris — voluntary and updated every 5 years

quiz_questions.question_text: The Paris Agreement is different from Kyoto because it:

options.text: Includes all nations (not just developed), uses voluntary NDCs rather than binding targets, and updates every 5 years

quiz_questions.question_text: A criticism of the Paris Agreement is that:

options.text: Current NDCs are insufficient to limit warming to 1.5°C or even 2°C; voluntary nature means no enforcement mechanism

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'ratchet mechanism' in the Paris Agreement means:

options.text: Countries are expected to increase their climate ambition over time (each successive NDC should be stronger)

quiz_questions.question_text: Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) means:

options.text: All nations share climate responsibility, but developed nations bear greater obligation due to historical emissions and greater capacity

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate finance ($100 billion/year goal) is meant to help:

options.text: Developing nations mitigate emissions and adapt to climate impacts

quiz_questions.question_text: The Green Climate Fund was established to:

options.text: Channel climate finance to developing countries for mitigation and adaptation projects

quiz_questions.question_text: The US withdrew from the Paris Agreement under _____ and rejoined under _____.
COP (Conference of the Parties) refers to:

options.text: Annual UN climate negotiations where nations discuss climate action (e.g., COP28 in Dubai, 2023)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Montreal Protocol (1987) on ozone shows that:

options.text: Global environmental treaties can be highly successful (CFC reduction → ozone layer recovery on track)
Only small problems can be solved

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon markets created under international agreements allow:

options.text: Countries or companies to trade emission credits, theoretically reducing costs of emission reductions

quiz_questions.question_text: Article 6 of the Paris Agreement deals with:

options.text: International carbon markets and cooperative approaches to emission reductions

quiz_questions.question_text: Loss and Damage refers to:

options.text: Impacts of climate change that go beyond what can be adapted to, especially in vulnerable nations

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, students should compare Kyoto and Paris in terms of:

options.text: Scope (who participates), binding vs. voluntary, targets, mechanisms, and effectiveness

quiz_questions.question_text: The ultimate success of climate agreements depends on:

options.text: Whether nations actually implement their commitments and increase ambition fast enough to limit warming

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Climate Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Climate Policy

EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS)

World's largest carbon market. Cap-and-trade covering ~40% of EU emissions. Reduced covered emissions ~35% since 2005.

California's Climate Programs

State-level action: cap-and-trade, 100% clean electricity by 2045, EV mandates, building codes. Often ahead of federal policy.

China's Dual Role

Largest emitter (~30% of global CO₂) but also largest renewable energy investor. Launched world's largest ETS in 2021.

flashcards.definition: European Union Emissions Trading System: world's largest cap-and-trade carbon market since 2005.
Comprehensive state-level climate policy including cap-and-trade, 100% clean electricity target, and EV mandates.
Largest global emitter (~30% of CO₂) but also the world's largest investor in renewable energy.
Tax on imports from countries with weaker climate policies to prevent 'carbon leakage' (EU CBAM).
Climate policies by states, cities, and regions — often more ambitious than national governments.

quiz_questions.question_text: The EU ETS is the world's largest:

options.text: Cap-and-trade carbon market (covering ~40% of EU emissions since 2005)

quiz_questions.question_text: The EU ETS has reduced covered emissions by approximately _____ since 2005.
An early challenge for the EU ETS was:

options.text: Over-allocation of free allowances, which kept carbon prices too low to drive significant change

quiz_questions.question_text: California has set a goal of _____ clean electricity by 2045.
California's climate policy is significant because it:

options.text: Demonstrates that aggressive climate action and economic growth can coexist (5th largest economy globally)

quiz_questions.question_text: China emits approximately _____ of global CO₂.
Despite being the largest emitter, China leads in:

options.text: Renewable energy investment and deployment (largest solar, wind, and EV capacity globally)

quiz_questions.question_text: China launched the world's largest ETS in 2021 covering:

options.text: The power sector initially (then expanding to other sectors)

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon leakage occurs when:

options.text: Industries relocate to countries with weaker climate policies to avoid carbon costs

quiz_questions.question_text: The EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) addresses leakage by:

options.text: Taxing imports based on their carbon content — equalizing costs for domestic and foreign producers

quiz_questions.question_text: India's International Solar Alliance promotes:

options.text: Global cooperation to mobilize investment in solar energy, especially in developing countries

quiz_questions.question_text: The UK's Climate Change Act (2008) was significant because it:

options.text: Legally mandated 80% emission reduction by 2050 (later amended to net-zero), with five-year carbon budgets

quiz_questions.question_text: Cities signing the C40 coalition pledge to:

options.text: Meet the most ambitious goals of the Paris Agreement through urban climate action

quiz_questions.question_text: New Zealand included _____ in its ETS, a first for any country.
Costa Rica aims to be carbon-neutral by:

options.text: 2050 (with a plan centered on renewable electricity, reforestation, and green transport)

quiz_questions.question_text: The effectiveness of climate policy depends most on:

options.text: Implementation, enforcement, adequate ambition, and political will to maintain long-term commitments

quiz_questions.question_text: Fossil fuel subsidy reform is critical because:

options.text: Global fossil fuel subsidies ($7+ trillion/year including externalities) undermine climate policy by making dirty energy artificially cheap

quiz_questions.question_text: These case studies demonstrate that:

options.text: Multiple policy approaches (carbon pricing, regulations, investment, cooperation) are needed at all levels

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, climate policy questions test ability to:

options.text: Evaluate policy effectiveness, compare approaches, analyze trade-offs, and connect policies to emission outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: The key takeaway from climate policy case studies is:

options.text: Policy design matters enormously — ambition, enforcement, coverage, and equity determine whether agreements translate to real emission reductions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Climate Models
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Climate Models

Key Concepts

  • Climate models (GCMs): Computer simulations of Earth's climate system using physics equations. Multiple models run for different emission scenarios.
  • Emission scenarios (RCPs/SSPs): RCP 2.6 (aggressive mitigation) to RCP 8.5 (business as usual). SSPs update these.
  • Model outputs: Temperature projections range from +1.5°C to +4.5°C+ by 2100 depending on emissions.
  • Carbon budget: Total CO₂ that can still be emitted to stay below a temperature target (~400 Gt CO₂ remaining for 1.5°C).

flashcards.definition: Computer simulation of Earth's climate system using physical equations for atmosphere, ocean, land, and ice.
Scenarios of future greenhouse gas concentrations used in climate modeling: 2.6 (low) to 8.5 (high).
Updated scenarios combining socioeconomic development with emission pathways for climate projections.
Temperature increase expected from doubling atmospheric CO₂; estimated 2.5–4.0°C.
Total remaining CO₂ emissions allowed to stay below a specific temperature target.

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate models (GCMs) simulate Earth's climate using:

options.text: Physical equations governing atmosphere, ocean, land surface, and ice interactions

quiz_questions.question_text: RCP 2.6 represents a scenario with:

options.text: Aggressive mitigation — emissions peak soon and decline, limiting warming to ~1.5-2°C by 2100

quiz_questions.question_text: RCP 8.5 represents:

options.text: Business as usual (high emissions continue, leading to ~4-5°C+ warming by 2100)

quiz_questions.question_text: Temperature projections for 2100 range from:

options.text: +1.5°C to +4.5°C+ above pre-industrial (depending on emission scenario)

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate sensitivity is defined as the warming from:

options.text: Doubling of atmospheric CO₂ concentration (from 280 to 560 ppm); estimated at 2.5–4.0°C

quiz_questions.question_text: The remaining carbon budget for 1.5°C is approximately:

options.text: ~400 Gt CO₂ (at current emission rates, ~10 years' worth)

quiz_questions.question_text: At current emission rates (~40 Gt CO₂/year), the 1.5°C budget runs out in about:
Climate models are validated by:

options.text: Comparing model predictions against observed historical climate data (hindcasting)

quiz_questions.question_text: Climate models have accurately predicted:

options.text: Global temperature trends since the 1970s; models from the 1990s align well with observed warming

quiz_questions.question_text: Uncertainty in climate models comes from:

options.text: Unknown future emissions, natural variability, and incomplete understanding of some feedback mechanisms

quiz_questions.question_text: SSPs (Shared Socioeconomic Pathways) differ from RCPs by including:

options.text: Socioeconomic factors (population, GDP, governance, technology) that affect both emissions and adaptive capacity

quiz_questions.question_text: Positive feedback in climate models amplifies warming through:

options.text: Ice-albedo feedback, water vapor amplification, permafrost methane release, and cloud changes

quiz_questions.question_text: Tipping points in models represent:

options.text: Thresholds where small additional warming triggers large, irreversible changes (ice sheet collapse, Amazon dieback)

quiz_questions.question_text: The IPCC uses _____ models from research groups worldwide to improve reliability.

options.text: Dozens of independent models (ensemble approach reduces uncertainty)

quiz_questions.question_text: Downscaling refers to:

options.text: Converting global model outputs to regional/local scale projections (necessary for local adaptation planning)

quiz_questions.question_text: Net-zero emissions means:

options.text: Emissions produced are balanced by an equal amount of CO₂ removal (net effect = zero addition to atmosphere)

quiz_questions.question_text: To stay below 1.5°C, global emissions must reach net-zero by approximately:
Climate models show that even with aggressive mitigation:

options.text: Some additional warming is locked in due to past emissions and heat already in the ocean (adaptation will be needed regardless)

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP FRQ involving climate models, students should:

options.text: Interpret model outputs, compare scenarios, explain uncertainty, and connect projections to policy decisions

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding climate models is essential because they:

options.text: Provide the scientific basis for policy decisions affecting 8 billion people and every ecosystem on Earth

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: History of Environmental Movements
summary_sections.content_html:
History of Environmental Movements

Modern environmentalism grew from conservation and preservation movements of the 19th century.

Key Milestones

  • 1850s–1900s: John Muir (preservation), Gifford Pinchot (conservation/wise use), creation of first national parks.
  • 1962: Rachel Carson's Silent Spring — exposed pesticide dangers, sparked modern environmental movement.
  • 1970: First Earth Day (20 million participants). EPA established. Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act followed.
  • 1980s–present: Environmental justice movement, global issues (ozone, climate), sustainability focus.

flashcards.definition: Philosophy of protecting wilderness in its natural state (John Muir, Sierra Club).
Philosophy of managing natural resources for sustainable human use (Gifford Pinchot, 'wise use').
Rachel Carson's 1962 book exposing DDT's ecological damage; catalyzed the modern environmental movement and led to DDT ban.
First celebrated April 22, 1970; 20 million participants; led directly to the creation of the EPA.
Movement addressing the disproportionate environmental burdens on minority and low-income communities.

quiz_questions.question_text: The preservation vs. conservation debate was between:

options.text: John Muir (preservation: protect wilderness untouched) vs. Gifford Pinchot (conservation: manage for sustainable use)

quiz_questions.question_text: Rachel Carson's Silent Spring (1962) focused on the dangers of:

options.text: Pesticides (especially DDT) and their accumulation in food chains (bioaccumulation/biomagnification)

quiz_questions.question_text: The first Earth Day (April 22, 1970) attracted approximately:
The EPA (Environmental Protection Agency) was established in:

options.text: 1970 (by President Nixon in response to growing public concern)

quiz_questions.question_text: The environmental justice movement highlights that:

options.text: Minority and low-income communities disproportionately bear the burden of pollution and environmental hazards

quiz_questions.question_text: The Cuyahoga River fire (1969) in Cleveland helped spark environmental legislation because:

options.text: A river catching fire (from industrial pollution) was so shocking it galvanized public demand for clean water laws

quiz_questions.question_text: Gifford Pinchot's 'wise use' philosophy advocated:

options.text: Scientifically managed resource use for sustained yield and human benefit

quiz_questions.question_text: John Muir founded the _____ in 1892.
The first national park in the world was:
Aldo Leopold's 'land ethic' argued that:

options.text: Humans have an ethical duty to protect the integrity of ecological communities (expanded ethics to include land)

quiz_questions.question_text: The environmental movement of the 1960s–70s was influenced by:

options.text: Multiple factors: Silent Spring, pollution disasters, nuclear testing concerns, and a broader social justice climate

quiz_questions.question_text: Love Canal (1978) was significant because:

options.text: The discovery of toxic waste buried under a neighborhood led to evacuation and the creation of Superfund (CERCLA)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Bhopal disaster (1984) in India involved:

options.text: A chemical plant leak (methyl isocyanate) that killed thousands — highlighting industrial safety and environmental justice

quiz_questions.question_text: The modern environmental movement has expanded to include:

options.text: Climate change, biodiversity loss, environmental justice, sustainability, and global governance

quiz_questions.question_text: Indigenous peoples' environmental knowledge is increasingly recognized as:

options.text: Valuable for conservation and resource management (traditional ecological knowledge has sustained ecosystems for millennia)

quiz_questions.question_text: The relationship between poverty and environmental degradation is:

options.text: Complex and bidirectional — poverty can drive overexploitation, and degradation deepens poverty (vicious cycle)

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental movements in developing nations often focus on:

options.text: Access to clean water, air quality, land rights, corporate accountability, and resistance to resource extraction

quiz_questions.question_text: Youth climate movements (e.g., Fridays for Future) demonstrate that:

options.text: Young people are powerful advocates demanding intergenerational justice and faster climate action

quiz_questions.question_text: Corporate environmentalism emerged as companies realized:

options.text: Environmental responsibility can be both profitable and demanded by consumers and investors (ESG, green branding)

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, students should trace the evolution of environmental thought from:

options.text: Preservation → conservation → environmental regulation → environmental justice → global sustainability

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Environmental Laws & Regulations
summary_sections.content_html:
Major US Environmental Laws

  • NEPA (1970): Requires Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) for federal projects.
  • Clean Air Act (1970, amended 1990): Regulates air pollutants; NAAQS standards; cap-and-trade for SO₂.
  • Clean Water Act (1972): Regulates point-source water pollution; NPDES permits; goal: fishable, swimmable waters.
  • Endangered Species Act (1973): Protects listed species and their critical habitat.
  • RCRA (1976): Regulates hazardous waste from generation to disposal (cradle-to-grave).
  • CERCLA/Superfund (1980): Cleans up contaminated sites; 'polluter pays' principle.

flashcards.definition: National Environmental Policy Act (1970): requires Environmental Impact Statements for major federal projects.
Federal law (1970) setting National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for criteria pollutants.
Federal law (1972) regulating point-source water pollution through NPDES permits.
Federal law (1973) protecting listed threatened and endangered species and their critical habitat.
Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (1980): cleans up contaminated sites using 'polluter pays' principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: NEPA requires federal agencies to prepare _____ for major projects affecting the environment.

options.text: An Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) assessing potential environmental effects

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Air Act established _____ for six criteria air pollutants.
The six criteria air pollutants under the Clean Air Act are:

options.text: PM, ground-level ozone, CO, SO₂, NO₂, and lead

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Air Act's acid rain program used _____ to reduce SO₂ emissions.

options.text: Cap-and-trade (market-based approach that achieved reductions faster and cheaper than expected)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Water Act primarily regulates _____ pollution.

options.text: Point-source (from identifiable discharge points like pipes, with NPDES permits)

quiz_questions.question_text: Non-point source pollution (runoff) is _____ regulated than point source.

options.text: Less strictly (harder to identify and control; addressed through voluntary programs and BMPs)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Endangered Species Act protects:

options.text: Species listed as threatened or endangered AND their critical habitat

quiz_questions.question_text: RCRA regulates hazardous waste:

options.text: From generation through transportation, treatment, storage, and disposal ('cradle to grave')

quiz_questions.question_text: CERCLA (Superfund) was created to:

options.text: Clean up existing contaminated sites and hold liable parties responsible ('polluter pays')

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'polluter pays' principle means:

options.text: The party responsible for contamination bears the cost of cleanup and remediation

quiz_questions.question_text: The Safe Drinking Water Act regulates:

options.text: Contaminant levels in public drinking water systems (sets Maximum Contaminant Levels, MCLs)

quiz_questions.question_text: FIFRA (Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act) regulates:

options.text: Pesticide registration, sale, distribution, and use in the United States

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental regulations are enforced through:

options.text: Permits, monitoring, reporting requirements, inspections, fines, and criminal penalties

quiz_questions.question_text: Cost-benefit analysis in environmental regulation involves:

options.text: Weighing the economic costs of regulation against the environmental and health benefits

quiz_questions.question_text: Critics of environmental regulation argue they:

options.text: Can be costly to businesses and may reduce competitiveness (though benefits often exceed costs)

quiz_questions.question_text: Supporters argue environmental regulations:

options.text: Protect public health, save costs from pollution-related damages, and often drive innovation (Porter hypothesis)

quiz_questions.question_text: Regulatory capture occurs when:

options.text: Regulated industries gain excessive influence over the agencies meant to regulate them

quiz_questions.question_text: The Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA, reformed 2016) regulates:

options.text: Chemical substances in commerce — reforms required EPA to evaluate existing chemicals and gave authority to restrict or ban them

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, students should know the purpose and key provision of:

options.text: NEPA, Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, ESA, RCRA, and CERCLA (and compare their approaches)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most effective environmental laws combine:

options.text: Clear standards, monitoring/enforcement, market incentives, and public participation

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: International Environmental Policy
summary_sections.content_html:
International Environmental Policy

Key Agreements

  • Stockholm Conference (1972): First major UN conference on the environment; established UNEP.
  • CITES (1975): Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species — regulates wildlife trade.
  • Montreal Protocol (1987): Phased out CFCs; ozone layer recovering — most successful environmental treaty.
  • CBD (1992): Convention on Biological Diversity — conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity.
  • Kunming-Montreal Framework (2022): '30 by 30' — protect 30% of land and sea by 2030.

flashcards.definition: United Nations Environment Programme, established 1972 at the Stockholm Conference to coordinate global environmental action.
1987 treaty phasing out CFCs and other ozone-depleting substances; ozone layer now recovering.
Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species: regulates and monitors international wildlife trade.
1992 treaty for conservation and sustainable use of biodiversity; includes Kunming-Montreal '30 by 30' framework.
Goal from 2022 Kunming-Montreal Framework to protect 30% of Earth's land and ocean by 2030.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 1972 Stockholm Conference was significant as the:

options.text: First major UN conference specifically on the human environment; established UNEP

quiz_questions.question_text: UNEP's role is to:

options.text: Coordinate international environmental action, provide scientific guidance, and support environmental programs

quiz_questions.question_text: CITES regulates:

options.text: International trade in endangered species of wild animals and plants (permits required)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Montreal Protocol is considered the most successful environmental treaty because:

options.text: CFC production dropped >99%, and the ozone layer is on track to recover by ~2065

quiz_questions.question_text: The Biodiversity Convention (CBD, 1992) aims to:

options.text: Conserve biodiversity, promote sustainable use, and ensure fair sharing of genetic resource benefits

quiz_questions.question_text: The 30 by 30 target from the Kunming-Montreal Framework (2022) aims to:

options.text: Protect 30% of Earth's land and 30% of ocean by 2030

quiz_questions.question_text: Currently, approximately _____ of land and _____ of ocean is protected.
The Basel Convention (1989) regulates:

options.text: Transboundary movement of hazardous wastes (preventing dumping in developing countries)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Rotterdam Convention requires _____ for trade in hazardous chemicals.

options.text: Prior Informed Consent (importing countries must agree before receiving hazardous chemicals or pesticides)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Stockholm Convention (2001) targets:

options.text: Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) — banning or restricting the 'dirty dozen' and others

quiz_questions.question_text: International environmental agreements face the challenge of:

options.text: Sovereignty — nations may resist binding commitments and there is no global enforcement authority

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'tragedy of the commons' in international context means:

options.text: Nations may overexploit shared global resources (oceans, atmosphere) because no single nation bears the full cost

quiz_questions.question_text: Free-rider problems in environmental treaties occur when:

options.text: Some nations benefit from others' environmental efforts without contributing (undermining collective action)

quiz_questions.question_text: The relationship between trade and environment is:

options.text: Complex — trade can spread pollution (pollution havens) but also spread clean technology and raise living standards

quiz_questions.question_text: The World Trade Organization and environmental agreements sometimes conflict because:

options.text: WTO rules promoting free trade may clash with environmental regulations seen as trade barriers

quiz_questions.question_text: Multilateral Environmental Agreements (MEAs) work best when they have:

options.text: Clear targets, monitoring mechanisms, financial support for developing countries, and compliance incentives

quiz_questions.question_text: South-South cooperation in environmental policy involves:

options.text: Developing countries sharing knowledge, technology, and strategies for environmental challenges

quiz_questions.question_text: The principle of Precautionary Approach states:

options.text: Where there are threats of serious damage, lack of full scientific certainty should not postpone cost-effective preventive measures

quiz_questions.question_text: Debt-for-nature swaps involve:

options.text: Reducing a country's foreign debt in exchange for investments in domestic conservation programs

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, international environmental policy requires understanding:

options.text: Treaty purposes, effectiveness, challenges (sovereignty, compliance, equity), and connections to environmental outcomes

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Environmental Justice
summary_sections.content_html:
Environmental Justice

Environmental justice is the fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, income, or national origin with respect to environmental laws, regulations, and policies.

Key Issues

  • Hazardous waste facilities disproportionately sited in minority and low-income communities.
  • Flint, MI water crisis (2014): lead contamination from changed water source; primarily affecting Black residents.
  • Cancer Alley, Louisiana: 85-mile industrial corridor with elevated cancer rates in Black communities.
  • Executive Order 12898 (1994): required agencies to address environmental justice.

flashcards.definition: Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, income, or origin in environmental decisions.
Disproportionate siting of environmentally hazardous facilities in communities of color.
2014–present crisis: lead contamination from changed water source in Flint, MI, disproportionately affecting Black residents.
85-mile stretch along the Mississippi River in Louisiana with concentrated petrochemical plants and elevated cancer rates in predominantly Black communities.
1994 presidential order requiring federal agencies to identify and address disproportionate environmental and health effects on minority and low-income populations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental justice is defined as:

options.text: Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, income, or national origin in environmental matters

quiz_questions.question_text: Studies have shown that hazardous waste facilities are disproportionately located in:
The landmark 1982 Warren County, NC protest was significant because it:

options.text: Was the first major environmental justice protest (opposing a PCB landfill in a predominantly Black community) — sparked the EJ movement

quiz_questions.question_text: The Flint, MI water crisis involved:

options.text: Switching water sources to save money, which corroded pipes and leached lead into drinking water (primarily affecting Black residents)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cancer Alley in Louisiana demonstrates:

options.text: Environmental racism: concentrated petrochemical industries in a predominantly Black corridor → elevated cancer and respiratory disease rates

quiz_questions.question_text: Executive Order 12898 (1994) required:

options.text: Federal agencies to identify and address disproportionately high environmental and health effects on minority and low-income populations

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental justice issues are not limited to race but also include:

options.text: Income level, indigenous status, immigrant communities, rural communities, and other marginalized groups

quiz_questions.question_text: NIMBY ('Not In My Back Yard') is related to EJ because:

options.text: Wealthy, politically powerful communities successfully push unwanted facilities to less powerful communities

quiz_questions.question_text: Indigenous communities face environmental justice concerns including:

options.text: Natural resource extraction on their lands, contaminated water, broken treaties, and loss of cultural resources

quiz_questions.question_text: The intersection of environmental justice and climate change shows:

options.text: Climate impacts disproportionately affect low-income communities and communities of color (heat islands, flood zones, less access to adaptation resources)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cumulative impacts refer to:

options.text: The combined health effects of multiple environmental hazards in one community (air, water, soil, social stressors)

quiz_questions.question_text: Community-based participatory research in EJ involves:

options.text: Residents of affected communities as partners in designing and conducting research on environmental health threats

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental gentrification occurs when:

options.text: Environmental cleanup or green amenities raise property values, displacing the original community that suffered the pollution

quiz_questions.question_text: Title VI of the Civil Rights Act applies to EJ because:

options.text: It prohibits discrimination in federally funded programs — EPA can investigate claims of environmental discrimination

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2021 Justice40 Initiative aims to:

options.text: Deliver 40% of benefits from federal climate and environment investments to disadvantaged communities

quiz_questions.question_text: Global environmental justice includes the concept that:

options.text: Developing nations suffer climate impacts caused largely by developed nations' historical emissions (climate injustice)

quiz_questions.question_text: Health disparities in EJ communities include:

options.text: Higher rates of asthma, cancer, lead poisoning, and other pollution-related diseases compared to wealthier, whiter communities

quiz_questions.question_text: The solution to environmental injustice requires:

options.text: Meaningful community participation in decisions, equitable enforcement of laws, investments in cleanup, and addressing root causes of inequality

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, environmental justice questions require analyzing:

options.text: How race, income, and power affect distribution of environmental burdens and benefits with specific examples

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental justice is fundamentally about:

options.text: The intersection of environmental protection, civil rights, and social equity — environment and justice are inseparable

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies: Policy Implementation
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies: Policy Implementation

Success: Acid Rain Program

Clean Air Act cap-and-trade reduced SO₂ emissions ~90% from 1990 levels — ahead of schedule and under budget. Lakes and forests recovering.

Ongoing Challenge: Superfund

Over 1,300 sites on the National Priorities List. Cleanup slow and expensive; ~400 sites fully cleaned since 1980. Environmental justice concerns.

Controversial: ESA Implementation

Spotted owl vs. logging in Pacific Northwest. Species recovery success stories but habitat-economy conflicts persist.

flashcards.definition: Clean Air Act Title IV cap-and-trade that reduced SO₂ emissions ~90% — one of the most successful environmental regulations.
National Priorities List of ~1,300+ contaminated sites; cleanup has been slow and expensive since 1980.
Listing northern spotted owl under ESA led to logging restrictions in Pacific Northwest — economy vs. conservation debate.
Policy approach of 'learn by doing' — implementing, monitoring, and adjusting management strategies based on results.
Measured by whether laws achieve their environmental goals, at what cost, and with what distributional effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Acid Rain Program reduced SO₂ emissions by approximately:

options.text: ~90% from 1990 levels (ahead of schedule and under budget)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Acid Rain Program succeeded because:

options.text: Cap-and-trade allowed flexibility — companies found cheapest ways to reduce emissions; clear cap ensured total reductions

quiz_questions.question_text: Acid rain damaged:

options.text: Lakes (acidification killing fish), forests (nutrient leaching), and buildings/monuments (dissolving limestone)

quiz_questions.question_text: Superfund has fully cleaned approximately _____ of 1,300+ sites since 1980.

options.text: ~400 sites (cleanup is slow, complex, and very expensive)

quiz_questions.question_text: Superfund cleanup is complicated by:

options.text: Multiple responsible parties, complex contamination, high costs, community opposition, and environmental justice concerns

quiz_questions.question_text: The spotted owl controversy illustrates the tension between:

options.text: Endangered species protection (ESA) and economic interests (timber industry and jobs)

quiz_questions.question_text: The ESA has been successful in that:

options.text: The majority of listed species are stable or improving (bald eagles, gray wolves, grizzly bears among recovery successes)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Water Act's success is measured by:

options.text: Reduction in point-source pollution — but non-point source pollution and legacy contamination remain major challenges

quiz_questions.question_text: The Chesapeake Bay cleanup demonstrates:

options.text: The difficulty of addressing non-point source pollution from agriculture, development, and atmospheric deposition across multiple states

quiz_questions.question_text: The Montreal Protocol's implementation shows that phaseouts work when:

options.text: Affordable substitutes are available, there's industry cooperation, and developing nations receive financial and technical support

quiz_questions.question_text: Environmental regulation's effect on the economy is generally:

options.text: Modest overall costs with significant health and environmental benefits — often driving innovation (e.g., catalytic converters became a new industry)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Clean Air Act's benefits have been estimated at _____ its costs.

options.text: 30× or more (trillions in health benefits vs. billions in compliance costs)

quiz_questions.question_text: Enforcement challenges for environmental laws include:

options.text: Limited budgets, political pressure, difficulty monitoring non-point sources, and the revolving door between regulators and industry

quiz_questions.question_text: Adaptive management in environmental policy involves:

options.text: Implementing policies, monitoring outcomes, and adjusting strategies based on what's working and what isn't

quiz_questions.question_text: International cooperation in enforcement is needed because:

options.text: Pollution crosses borders, wildlife trafficking is global, and many environmental problems are transboundary

quiz_questions.question_text: The Deepwater Horizon spill (2010) led to:

options.text: Strengthened offshore drilling regulations, $20+ billion in fines/cleanup, and highlighted gaps in oil spill preparedness

quiz_questions.question_text: Cost-effectiveness varies across environmental tools:

options.text: Market-based tools (cap-and-trade, taxes) are often cheaper per unit of reduction than command-and-control regulations

quiz_questions.question_text: Public participation in environmental decisions is important because:

options.text: It ensures affected communities have input, improves decisions with local knowledge, and strengthens legitimacy

quiz_questions.question_text: For AP, policy implementation case studies require:

options.text: Evaluating what worked, what didn't, comparing approaches, and applying lessons to new situations

quiz_questions.question_text: The key lesson from these case studies is that environmental policy:

options.text: Is iterative — success depends on design, enforcement, funding, public support, and willingness to adapt based on results

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Prep: Policy Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Prep: Policy Analysis

FRQ Strategy

  • Identify the problem: What environmental issue is being addressed?
  • Describe the policy: What does the law/regulation do? Who enforces it?
  • Evaluate effectiveness: Has it achieved its goals? What evidence?
  • Analyze trade-offs: Economic costs vs. environmental/health benefits.
  • Propose improvements: How could the policy be strengthened or reformed?

Key Comparisons

Command-and-control vs. market-based approaches. Federal vs. state vs. international. Point source vs. non-point source. Mitigation vs. adaptation.

flashcards.definition: Government sets specific standards or technology requirements that regulated entities must meet.
Use economic incentives (taxes, cap-and-trade, subsidies) to achieve environmental goals at lower cost.
Systematic comparison of the costs and benefits of a policy to determine if benefits justify costs.
Command-and-control provides certainty of outcome; market-based provides cost-efficiency — often used together.
Problem identification → policy description → effectiveness evaluation → trade-off analysis → improvement proposals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Command-and-control regulations specify:

options.text: Specific standards, limits, or technologies that must be used (e.g., emission limits, required technology)

quiz_questions.question_text: Market-based approaches to environmental policy include:

options.text: Carbon taxes, cap-and-trade, subsidies for clean energy, and deposit-refund systems

quiz_questions.question_text: An advantage of market-based approaches is:

options.text: Greater cost-effectiveness — allows companies to find the cheapest way to meet environmental goals

quiz_questions.question_text: An advantage of command-and-control is:
